Originally, in the {{ill|Old Kingdom|de|Altes Reich|ja|古王国|zh|古王国}}, this role was reserved for the king’s eldest son; later, the Sem priest embodied the king’s son as “Observer at the ritual purification of the deceased,” linking directly to the deity {{ill|Sem|de|Sem|ja|セム|zh|塞姆}}. === Activities of the Sem priest === The Sem priest officiated the {{ill|Opening of the Mouth ceremony|de|Mundöffnungszeremonie|ja|口開儀式|zh|开口仪式}}, depicted in panther pelt, and mediated between king and gods. He dressed the king, allocated offerings, and during {{ill|Sed festival|de|Sedfest|ja|セド祭|zh|Sed节}} processions renewed royal power. In the temple vestibule of {{ill|Pepi II|de|Pepi II.|ja|ペピ 2世|zh|佩皮 二世}}, the west wall shows the Sem offering linen, incense, and oil to the shrine gods, confirming the king as “First of the living”: {{Quote|The gods come, bringing ‘life, power, and health for the son of his body Nebmaatre’ and promise: ‘He celebrates the New Year festival with a broad heart, renews the months in the Nehebkau festival, and performs many Sed festivals on the throne like Re.’|West wall of Pepi II|ref=Gustave Jéquier: ''Le monument funéraire de Pepi II, Service des Antiquités de l'Égypte, Bd. 2: Le temple''. Institut Français d'Archéologie Orientale, Cairo 1938, Plates 50 & 52.}} In Greco-Roman liturgy, {{ill|Seth|de|Seth|ja|セト|zh|赛特}} fled {{ill|Thoth|de|Thot|ja|トト|zh|托特}} and {{ill|Anubis|de|Anubis|ja|アヌビス|zh|阿努比斯}}, shape-shifted into a panther, and was slain by Anubis. Anubis then entered Osiris’s embalming hall wearing the flayed pelt, declaring: “Seth is here.” == See also == * {{ill|Sem-em-iritef|de|Sem-em-iritef|ja|Sem-em-iritef|zh|Sem-em-iritef}} * {{ill|Setem-atef-Usir|de|Setem-atef-Usir|ja|Setem-atef-Usir|zh|Setem-atef-Usir}} * {{ill|Min-Fest|de|Min-Fest|ja|Min-Fest|zh|Min-Fest}} * {{ill|Ancient Egyptian administrative and functional titles|de|Altägyptische Beamten- und Funktionstitel|ja|古代エジプトの行政・官職称号|zh|古埃及行政与职能头衔}} == Literature == * Friedrich-Wilhelm Bissing, Hermann Kees: ''Das Re-Heiligtum des Königs Ne-Woser-Re (Rathures), Bd. 2''. Duncker, Berlin 1923, Plates 13, 18; pp. 68–69. * Hermann Kees: ''Das Priestertum im ägyptischen Staat vom Neuen Reich bis zur Spätzeit''. Brill, Leiden 1953. * Christian Leitz et al.: ''Lexikon der ägyptischen Götter und Götterbezeichnungen''. Bd. 6: “H̱ - s”. Peeters, Leuven 2002, ISBN 90-429-1151-4, p. 306. * Flinders Petrie: ''Abydos I + II''. Brian Yare Egyptology, Worcester 2005 (Reprint 1902). * Heinrich Schäfer: ''Die Mysterien des Osiris in Abydos unter König Sesostris III – Nach dem Denkstein …''. Hinrichs, Leipzig 1904, pp. 15–19. * William A. Ward: ''Index of Egyptian administrative and religious titles of the Middle Kingdom: with a glossary …''. American University of Beirut, Beirut 1982, p. 168, No. 1465. == Weblinks == * [http://www.egyptology.com/reeder/enigma/tekenu8.html The Sem priest as panther-pelt wielder] == Einzelnachweise == {{draft categories| [[Category:Https://es.wikipedia.org/wiki/]] [[Category:Https://fr.wikipedia.org/wiki/]] }} [[en::Sem priest]] [[sn:automatic wikipedia redirects]] [[sn:pages created from Interlanguage links]] [[fr:Pr%C3%AAtre-sem]] [[es:Sacerdote sem]] [[sn:Redirects to be cleared]] [[de:Sem (Priester)]][[ja:セム祭司]][[zh:瑟姆祭司]] [[Category:Priest Titles (Ancient Egypt)]] [[Category:translated pages]] [[ja:セムとムトネフェル]][[de:Sem und Mut-nefer]]

revision.text:
{{Infobox Hieroglyphen Zweispaltig |TITEL = Sem und Mut-nefer |NAME = G14-F35
Mut-nefer
''Mwt-nfr'' |NAME-ERWEITERT = [[Frühdynastische Periode (Ägypten)|Thinitenzeit]] |NAME2 = S29-G17
S29-X1-G17 |NAME2-ERWEITERT = [[Mittleres Reich]] |NAME3 = S29-G17-A1
S29-X1-G17-A1
Sem / Setem
''Sm / Stm'' |NAME3-ERWEITERT = [[Neues Reich]] |NAME4-ERWEITERT = Nebentitel |NAME4 = R8-S20
[[Chetemu-netjer]]
''Ḫtmw-nṯr''
''Siegelbewahrer (des) Gottes''
auch: ''Gottessiegler'' }} '''Sem''' (auch '''Setem''') ist im [[Altes Reich|Alten-]], [[Mittleres Reich|Mittleren-]] und in der Frühzeit des [[Neues Reich|Neuen Reiches]] hauptsächlich die [[Altes Ägypten|altägyptische]] Bezeichnung eines Funktionstitels vom „[[Hohepriester]] des [[Osiris]]“ und definierte sich über die Tätigkeit der Sem-Priesterschaft in den [[Totentempel]]n. Die genaue Übersetzung des in diesem Zusammenhang verwendeten Begriffs „Setem-Priester“ bleibt unklar. Ein [[Elfenbein]]täfelchen des [[Den (König)|Den]] ([[Frühdynastische Periode (Ägypten)|1. Dynastie]]) stellt den [[Ikonografie|ikonografisch]] ältesten Beleg für die Funktion des Titels Sem dar,Elisabeth Staehlin: ''Untersuchungen zur ägyptischen Tracht im Alten Reich''. Hessling, Berlin 1966, S. 66–67, Tafel XXXIV, Abbildung 38. der in jener Zeit als ''Mut-nefer'' noch an den [[Altägyptische Beamten- und Funktionstitel|Titel]] [[Tjet]] gekoppelt war. Der [[Ritus|Kult]] des Sem nahm eine Sonderstellung ein, da er ebenso Tätigkeiten der Position des „Hohepriester des [[Sameref (ägyptische Mythologie)|Sameref]]“ beinhaltete und die Grundlage für die Verehrung des [[Chontamenti|Osiris-Chontamenti]] in [[Abydos (Ägypten)|Abydos]] bildete. Von diesem Osiris-Tempelbezirk, der im Norden von Abydos lag, sind heute nur noch geringe Überreste vorhanden. Aus den [[Inschrift]]en abydenischer Denkmäler im Mittleren Reich geht hervor, dass sich die in Abydos tätigen Priester und Beamten zugleich als „Sa-meref“ und „Sem“ verstanden. == Hintergrund == === Rollenzuweisung des Sem-Priesters === [[Datei:Egypt.Osiris.statuette.01.jpg|mini|140px|Statue von Osiris im [[Ägyptisches Museum (Kairo)|Ägyptischen Museum in Kairo]]]] Der Sem-Priester symbolisiert hauptsächlich den fürsorgenden Sohn des Osiris im Kontext mit [[Horus]], der sich aus dem Totenkult ergibt. Damit repräsentiert der Sem-Priester den Charakter eines Sohnes, der seinen toten Vater versorgt. Die Sem-Priesterschaft übernimmt damit auf weltlicher Ebene die Aufgaben der [[Semu|Sem-Gottheiten]]. Als [[Pendant]] des göttlichen Sohns Sa-meref kann der Sem-Priester den König beleben, regenerieren und dessen Weg zu ewiger Herrschaft öffnen. Aus der Zeit des [[Thutmosis III.]] datiert die Inschrift des Neb-waui, „Hohepriester des Osiris und des Sa-meref“: {{Zitat|Ich versah mein erstes Amt im Tempel des Vaters Osiris. Ich war ein Sa-meref in der Prozedur des Goldhauses, in den Geheimnissen des Herrn von Abydos. Ich war geschickt bezüglich der Arme beim Schmücken der [[Statue|Gottesstatue]]: Ein Sem-Priester mit reinen Fingern. Alle Handwerker standen unter meiner Aufsicht: Sie geschahen durch mich.|Neb-waui|ref=Selke-Susan Eichler: ''Die Verwaltung des „Hauses des Amun“ in der 18. Dynastie''. Buske, Hamburg 2000, ISBN 3-87548-232-8, S. 191–192.}} Im weiteren Verlauf der [[18. Dynastie]] führten die „[[Wer-cherep-hemut|Hohepriester des Ptah]]“ zusätzlich den Titel des Sem-Priesters, da sie sich als Abbild des Gottes [[Iunmutef]] verstanden, weshalb sich entsprechend das [[Ornat]] der Sem-Priester änderte, das nun das [[Pantherfell]] und die [[Jugendlocke]] beinhaltete. In die Regierungszeit des [[Amenophis III.]] konnte der früheste Beleg für die Erweiterung des [[Memphis (Ägypten)|memphitischen]] Hohepriestertitels mit der Sem-Priestereigenschaft datiert werden. Eine Statue des Ptahmose, Sohn des [[Mencheper]], zeigt ihn in der Tracht des Iunmutef, ergänzt mit der Inschrift „Abbild des Iunmutef“. Ergänzend führte deshalb der Sem-Priester die Bezeichnung des „Königssohnes“ mit dem Titel „[[Horus Iunmutef|Horus Iunmutef]]“. Im Alten Reich oblag ursprünglich dem ältesten Sohn des Königs diese Aufgabe, die später der Sem-Priester als Verkörperung des Königsohnes in der Rolle des „Zusehenden bei der rituellen Reinigung der Verstorbenen“ übernahm und damit einen direkten Bezug zu der dem Osiris dienenden Gottheit [[Sem (ägyptische Mythologie)|Sem]] herstellte. === Tätigkeiten des Sem-Priesters === Sicher belegt ist seine Tätigkeit als Ritualpriester bezüglich der [[Mundöffnungszeremonie]], bei der er mit einem [[Pantherfell]] dargestellt ist. Der Sem-Priester übernahm unter anderem deshalb auch die Funktion des Vermittlers zwischen dem König und den Gottheiten. Zu den weiteren Aufgaben gehörte das Ankleiden des Königs, Opferzuweisungen an die Götter und Götterstatuen nebst Schreinen. Die Aktivitäten des Sem-Priesters konzentrierten sich im Zusammenhang mit den [[Prozession]]en des [[Sedfest]]es auf die Erhaltung und Erneuerung der Königsherrschaft. Im Vorraum des Tempels von [[Pepi II.]] ist auf der Westwand zu sehen, wie der ''Sem'' den „Göttern der Schreine“ das typische Dreifach-Opfer [[Flachsfaser|Leinen]], [[Weihrauch]] und [[Salböl]] darbringt, worauf die Götter wohlwollend den Thron bestätigen, wie auch den König als „Ersten aller Lebenden“: {{Zitat|Die Götter kommen, indem sie ‚Leben, Macht und Gesundheit für den Sohn seines Leibes Nebmaatre bringen‘ und verheißen ihm: ‚Er begeht das [[Neujahrsfest (Altes Ägypten)|Neujahrsfest]] weiten Herzens, er verjüngt die Monate im [[Nehebkau-Fest]] und er verbringt viele Sedfeste auf dem Thron wie Re.‘|Darstellung auf der Westwand des Pepi II.|ref=Gustave Jéquier: ''Le monument funéraire de Pepi II, Service des Antiquités de l'Égypte, Bd. 2: Le temple''. Institut Français d'Arch. Orientale, Kairo 1938, Tafeln 50 und 52.}} In der [[Liturgie]] der [[Ägypten in griechisch-römischer Zeit|griechisch-römischen Zeit]] verwandelte sich [[Seth (ägyptische Mythologie)|Seth]] auf der Flucht vor [[Thot]] und [[Anubis]] in einen [[Panther (ägyptische Mythologie)|Panther]], der von Anubis getötet wurde. Anschließend betrat Anubis mit dem abgezogenen Fell des Panthers die [[Balsamierungshalle]] des [[Osiris]] und sprach: „Seth ist hier“. == Siehe auch == * [[Sem-em-iritef]] * [[Setem-atef-Usir]] * [[Min-Fest]] * [[Altägyptische Beamten- und Funktionstitel]] == Literatur == * Friedrich-Wilhelm Bissing, Hermann Kees: ''Das Re-Heiligtum des Königs Ne-Woser-Re (Rathures), Bd. 2''. Duncker, Berlin 1923, Blatt 13, 18 und S. 68–69. * [[Hermann Kees]]: ''Das Priestertum im ägyptischen Staat vom Neuen Reich bis zur Spätzeit''. Brill, Leiden 1953. * [[Christian Leitz]] u. a.: ''[[Lexikon der ägyptischen Götter und Götterbezeichnungen]].'' Bd. 6: '' H̱ - s''. Peeters, Leuven 2002, ISBN 90-429-1151-4, S. 306 * [[Flinders Petrie]]: ''Abydos I + II''. Brian Yare Egyptology, Worcester 2005 (Reprint 1902). * [[Heinrich Schäfer (Ägyptologe)|Heinrich Schäfer]]: ''Die Mysterien des Osiris in Abydos unter König Sesostris III – Nach dem Denkstein des Oberschatzmeisters I-Cher-Nofret im Berliner Museum.'' Hinrichs, Leipzig 1904, S. 15–19. * [[William A. Ward]]: ''Index of Egyptian administrative and religious titles of the Middle Kingdom: with a glossary of words and phrases used.'' American University of Beirut, Beirut 1982, S. 168, Nr. 1465. == Weblinks == * [http://www.egyptology.com/reeder/enigma/tekenu8.html Der Sem-Priester als Tekenu-Pantherfellträger] == Einzelnachweise == [[Kategorie:Priestertitel (Altes Ägypten)]]

Alle Handwerker standen unter meiner Aufsicht: Sie geschahen durch mich.|Neb-waui|ref=Selke-Susan Eichler: ''Die Verwaltung des „Hauses des Amun“ in der 18. Dynastie''. Buske, Hamburg 2000, ISBN 3-87548-232-8, S. 191–192.}} Im weiteren Verlauf der [[18. Dynastie]] führten die „[[Wer-cherep-hemut|Hohepriester des Ptah]]“ zusätzlich den Titel des Sem-Priesters, da sie sich als Abbild des Gottes [[Iunmutef]] verstanden, weshalb sich entsprechend das [[Ornat]] der Sem-Priester änderte, das nun das [[Pantherfell]] und die [[Jugendlocke]] beinhaltete. Institut Français d'Arch. Orientale, Kairo 1938, Tafeln 50 und 52.}} In der [[Liturgie]] der [[Ägypten in griechisch-römischer Zeit|griechisch-römischen Zeit]] verwandelte sich [[Seth (ägyptische Mythologie)|Seth]] auf der Flucht vor [[Thot]] und [[Anubis]] in einen [[Panther (ägyptische Mythologie)|Panther]], der von Anubis getötet wurde. Duncker, Berlin 1923, Blatt 13, 18 und S. 68–69. * [[Hermann Kees]]: ''Das Priestertum im ägyptischen Staat vom Neuen Reich bis zur Spätzeit''. Hinrichs, Leipzig 1904, S. 15–19. * [[William A. Ward]]: ''Index of Egyptian administrative and religious titles of the Middle Kingdom: with a glossary of words and phrases used.'' {{Infobox hieroglyphs two-columns |TITEL = Sem and Mut-nefer |NAME = G14-F35
Mut-nefer
''Mwt-nfr'' |NAME-ERWEITERT = {{ill|Early Dynastic Period|de|Frühdynastische Periode (Ägypten)|ja|先王朝時代|zh|早期王朝時期}} |NAME2 = S29-G17
S29-X1-G17 |NAME2-ERWEITERT = {{ill|Middle Kingdom|de|Mittleres Reich|ja|中王国|zh|中王国}} |NAME3 = S29-G17-A1
S29-X1-G17-A1
Sem / Setem
''Sm / Stm'' |NAME3-ERWEITERT = {{ill|New Kingdom|de|Neues Reich|ja|新王国|zh|新王国}} |NAME4-ERWEITERT = Subtitle |NAME4 = R8-S20
{{ill|Chetemu-netjer|de|Chetemu-netjer|ja|チェテムネトジェル|zh|切特穆-内特哲}}
''Ḫtmw-nṯr''
''Seal Keeper of the God''
also: ''Divine Sigillator'' }} '''Sem''' (also '''Setem''') was, in the {{ill|Old Kingdom|de|Altes Reich|ja|古王国|zh|古王国}}, {{ill|Middle Kingdom|de|Mittleres Reich|ja|中王国|zh|中王国}}, and early {{ill|New Kingdom|de|Neues Reich|ja|新王国|zh|新王国}}, primarily the ancient Egyptian title for the “High Priest of {{ill|Osiris|de|Osiris|ja|オシリス|zh|奥西里斯}},” defined by the duties of the Sem priesthood in mortuary temples. The exact meaning of “Setem priest” in this context remains unclear. An ivory tag of {{ill|King Den|de|Den (Frühdynastische Periode)|ja|デン|zh|登}} (1st Dynasty) provides the oldest iconographic evidence for the title Sem,Elisabeth Staehlin: ''Untersuchungen zur ägyptischen Tracht im Alten Reich''. Hessling, Berlin 1966, pp. 66–67, Plate XXXIV, Figure 38. then called ''Mut-nefer'' and still linked to the title {{ill|Tjet|de|Tjet|ja|トジェト|zh|杰特}}. The Sem cult held a special position—it encompassed functions of the “High Priest of {{ill|Sameref|de|Sameref (ägyptische Mythologie)|ja|サメレフ|zh|萨梅里夫}}” and underpinned worship of {{ill|Osiris-Chontamenti|de|Osiris-Chontamenti|ja|オシリス・コントァメンティ|zh|奥西里斯-孔塔门提}} at {{ill|Abydos|de|Abydos|ja|アビュドス|zh|阿比多斯}}. == Background == === Role assignment of the Sem priest === [[File:Egypt.Osiris.statuette.01.jpg|mini|140px|Statue of Osiris, Egyptian Museum (Cairo)]] The Sem priest symbolizes the caring son of Osiris in relation to {{ill|Horus|de|Horus|ja|ホルス|zh|荷鲁斯}}, providing for his deceased father. On earth, the Sem priesthood assumed tasks of the {{ill|Semu|de|Semu|ja|セム神|zh|塞姆神}} deities. As counterpart to the divine son {{ill|Sa-meref|de|Sameref|ja|サメレフ|zh|萨梅里夫}}, the Sem priest could revive the king, regenerate him, and secure his eternal rule. An inscription from the reign of {{ill|Thutmose III|de|Thutmosis III.|ja|トトメス 3世|zh|图特摩斯 三世}} by {{ill|Neb-waui|de|Neb-waui|ja|ネブワウイ|zh|内布-瓦维}} reads: {{Quote|I performed my first office in the temple of Father Osiris. I served as Sa-meref in the Golden House rites, in the mysteries of the Lord of Abydos. I was skilled in adorning the god’s statue: a Sem priest with pure fingers. All craftsmen acted under my oversight.|Neb-waui|ref=Susan Eichler Selke: ''Die Verwaltung des „Hauses des Amun“ in der 18. Dynastie''. Buske, Hamburg 2000, pp. 191–192.}} During the {{ill|18th Dynasty|de|18. Dynastie|ja|第18王朝|zh|第十八王朝}}, the {{ill|High Priest of Ptah|de|Wer-cherep-hemut|ja|プタハの大司祭|zh|普塔赫大祭司}} also bore the title Sem priest, identifying with {{ill|Iunmutef|de|Iunmutef|ja|イウンムテフ|zh|尤努穆特夫}}. Their garb gained the panther pelt and sidelock. Under {{ill|Amenhotep III|de|Amenophis III.|ja|アメンホテプ 3世|zh|阿蒙霍特普 三世}}, we see the earliest Memphite high-priest title extended with Sem status: a statue of Ptahmose (“Image of Iunmutef”) son of Mencheper. The Sem priest also adopted the “King’s Son” title of {{ill|Horus Iunmutef|de|Horus Iunmutef|ja|ホルス・イウンムテフ|zh|荷鲁斯-尤努穆特夫}}. Originally, in the {{ill|Old Kingdom|de|Altes Reich|ja|古王国|zh|古王国}}, this role was reserved for the king’s eldest son; later, the Sem priest embodied the king’s son as “Observer at the ritual purification of the deceased,” linking directly to the deity {{ill|Sem|de|Sem|ja|セム|zh|塞姆}}. === Activities of the Sem priest === The Sem priest officiated the {{ill|Opening of the Mouth ceremony|de|Mundöffnungszeremonie|ja|口開儀式|zh|开口仪式}}, depicted in panther pelt, and mediated between king and gods. He dressed the king, allocated offerings, and during {{ill|Sed festival|de|Sedfest|ja|セド祭|zh|Sed节}} processions renewed royal power. In the temple vestibule of {{ill|Pepi II|de|Pepi II.|ja|ペピ 2世|zh|佩皮 二世}}, the west wall shows the Sem offering linen, incense, and oil to the shrine gods, confirming the king as “First of the living”: {{Quote|The gods come, bringing ‘life, power, and health for the son of his body Nebmaatre’ and promise: ‘He celebrates the New Year festival with a broad heart, renews the months in the Nehebkau festival, and performs many Sed festivals on the throne like Re.’|West wall of Pepi II|ref=Gustave Jéquier: ''Le monument funéraire de Pepi II, Service des Antiquités de l'Égypte, Bd. 2: Le temple''. Institut Français d'Archéologie Orientale, Cairo 1938, Plates 50 & 52.}} In Greco-Roman liturgy, {{ill|Seth|de|Seth|ja|セト|zh|赛特}} fled {{ill|Thoth|de|Thot|ja|トト|zh|托特}} and {{ill|Anubis|de|Anubis|ja|アヌビス|zh|阿努比斯}}, shape-shifted into a panther, and was slain by Anubis. Anubis then entered Osiris’s embalming hall wearing the flayed pelt, declaring: “Seth is here.” == See also == * {{ill|Sem-em-iritef|de|Sem-em-iritef|ja|Sem-em-iritef|zh|Sem-em-iritef}} * {{ill|Setem-atef-Usir|de|Setem-atef-Usir|ja|Setem-atef-Usir|zh|Setem-atef-Usir}} * {{ill|Min-Fest|de|Min-Fest|ja|Min-Fest|zh|Min-Fest}} * {{ill|Ancient Egyptian administrative and functional titles|de|Altägyptische Beamten- und Funktionstitel|ja|古代エジプトの行政・官職称号|zh|古埃及行政与职能头衔}} == Literature == * Friedrich-Wilhelm Bissing, Hermann Kees: ''Das Re-Heiligtum des Königs Ne-Woser-Re (Rathures), Bd. 2''. Duncker, Berlin 1923, Plates 13, 18; pp. 68–69. * Hermann Kees: ''Das Priestertum im ägyptischen Staat vom Neuen Reich bis zur Spätzeit''. Brill, Leiden 1953. * Christian Leitz et al.: ''Lexikon der ägyptischen Götter und Götterbezeichnungen''. Bd. 6: “H̱ - s”. Peeters, Leuven 2002, ISBN 90-429-1151-4, p. 306. * Flinders Petrie: ''Abydos I + II''. Brian Yare Egyptology, Worcester 2005 (Reprint 1902). * Heinrich Schäfer: ''Die Mysterien des Osiris in Abydos unter König Sesostris III – Nach dem Denkstein …''. Hinrichs, Leipzig 1904, pp. 15–19. * William A. Ward: ''Index of Egyptian administrative and religious titles of the Middle Kingdom: with a glossary …''. American University of Beirut, Beirut 1982, p. 168, No. 1465. == Weblinks == * [http://www.egyptology.com/reeder/enigma/tekenu8.html The Sem priest as panther-pelt wielder] == Einzelnachweise == {{draft categories| [[Category:Https://es.wikipedia.org/wiki/]] [[Category:Https://fr.wikipedia.org/wiki/]] }} [[en::Sem priest]] [[sn:automatic wikipedia redirects]] [[sn:pages created from Interlanguage links]] [[fr:Pr%C3%AAtre-sem]] [[es:Sacerdote sem]] [[sn:Redirects to be cleared]] [[de:Sem (Priester)]][[ja:セム祭司]][[zh:瑟姆祭司]] [[Category:Priest Titles (Ancient Egypt)]] [[Category:translated pages]] [[Category:Pages needing history import]] [[ja:セムとムトネフェル]][[de:Sem und Mut-nefer]]

{{ill|Chetemu-netjer|de|Chetemu-netjer|ja|チェテムネトジェル|zh|切特穆-内特哲}}
''Ḫtmw-nṯr''
''Seal Keeper of the God''
also: ''Divine Sigillator'' }} '''Sem''' (also '''Setem''') was, in the {{ill|Old Kingdom|de|Altes Reich|ja|古王国|zh|古王国}}, {{ill|Middle Kingdom|de|Mittleres Reich|ja|中王国|zh|中王国}}, and early {{ill|New Kingdom|de|Neues Reich|ja|新王国|zh|新王国}}, primarily the ancient Egyptian title for the “High Priest of {{ill|Osiris|de|Osiris|ja|オシリス|zh|奥西里斯}},” defined by the duties of the Sem priesthood in mortuary temples. The exact meaning of “Setem priest” in this context remains unclear. An ivory tag of {{ill|King Den|de|Den (Frühdynastische Periode)|ja|デン|zh|登}} (1st Dynasty) provides the oldest iconographic evidence for the title Sem,Elisabeth Staehlin: ''Untersuchungen zur ägyptischen Tracht im Alten Reich''. Hessling, Berlin 1966, pp. 66–67, Plate XXXIV, Figure 38. then called ''Mut-nefer'' and still linked to the title {{ill|Tjet|de|Tjet|ja|トジェト|zh|杰特}}. The Sem cult held a special position—it encompassed functions of the “High Priest of {{ill|Sameref|de|Sameref (ägyptische Mythologie)|ja|サメレフ|zh|萨梅里夫}}” and underpinned worship of {{ill|Osiris-Chontamenti|de|Osiris-Chontamenti|ja|オシリス・コントァメンティ|zh|奥西里斯-孔塔门提}} at {{ill|Abydos|de|Abydos|ja|アビュドス|zh|阿比多斯}}. == Background == === Role assignment of the Sem priest === [[File:Egypt.Osiris.statuette.01.jpg|mini|140px|Statue of Osiris, Egyptian Museum (Cairo)]] The Sem priest symbolizes the caring son of Osiris in relation to {{ill|Horus|de|Horus|ja|ホルス|zh|荷鲁斯}}, providing for his deceased father. On earth, the Sem priesthood assumed tasks of the {{ill|Semu|de|Semu|ja|セム神|zh|塞姆神}} deities. As counterpart to the divine son {{ill|Sa-meref|de|Sameref|ja|サメレフ|zh|萨梅里夫}}, the Sem priest could revive the king, regenerate him, and secure his eternal rule. An inscription from the reign of {{ill|Thutmose III|de|Thutmosis III.|ja|トトメス 3世|zh|图特摩斯 三世}} by {{ill|Neb-waui|de|Neb-waui|ja|ネブワウイ|zh|内布-瓦维}} reads: {{Quote|I performed my first office in the temple of Father Osiris. I served as Sa-meref in the Golden House rites, in the mysteries of the Lord of Abydos. I was skilled in adorning the god’s statue: a Sem priest with pure fingers. All craftsmen acted under my oversight.|Neb-waui|ref=Susan Eichler Selke: ''Die Verwaltung des „Hauses des Amun“ in der 18. Dynastie''. Buske, Hamburg 2000, pp. 191–192.}} During the {{ill|18th Dynasty|de|18. Dynastie|ja|第18王朝|zh|第十八王朝}}, the {{ill|High Priest of Ptah|de|Wer-cherep-hemut|ja|プタハの大司祭|zh|普塔赫大祭司}} also bore the title Sem priest, identifying with {{ill|Iunmutef|de|Iunmutef|ja|イウンムテフ|zh|尤努穆特夫}}. Their garb gained the panther pelt and sidelock. Under {{ill|Amenhotep III|de|Amenophis III.|ja|アメンホテプ 3世|zh|阿蒙霍特普 三世}}, we see the earliest Memphite high-priest title extended with Sem status: a statue of Ptahmose (“Image of Iunmutef”) son of Mencheper. The Sem priest also adopted the “King’s Son” title of {{ill|Horus Iunmutef|de|Horus Iunmutef|ja|ホルス・イウンムテフ|zh|荷鲁斯-尤努穆特夫}}. Originally, in the {{ill|Old Kingdom|de|Altes Reich|ja|古王国|zh|古王国}}, this role was reserved for the king’s eldest son; later, the Sem priest embodied the king’s son as “Observer at the ritual purification of the deceased,” linking directly to the deity {{ill|Sem|de|Sem|ja|セム|zh|塞姆}}. === Activities of the Sem priest === The Sem priest officiated the {{ill|Opening of the Mouth ceremony|de|Mundöffnungszeremonie|ja|口開儀式|zh|开口仪式}}, depicted in panther pelt, and mediated between king and gods. He dressed the king, allocated offerings, and during {{ill|Sed festival|de|Sedfest|ja|セド祭|zh|Sed节}} processions renewed royal power. In the temple vestibule of {{ill|Pepi II|de|Pepi II.|ja|ペピ 2世|zh|佩皮 二世}}, the west wall shows the Sem offering linen, incense, and oil to the shrine gods, confirming the king as “First of the living”: {{Quote|The gods come, bringing ‘life, power, and health for the son of his body Nebmaatre’ and promise: ‘He celebrates the New Year festival with a broad heart, renews the months in the Nehebkau festival, and performs many Sed festivals on the throne like Re.’|West wall of Pepi II|ref=Gustave Jéquier: ''Le monument funéraire de Pepi II, Service des Antiquités de l'Égypte, Bd. 2: Le temple''. Institut Français d'Archéologie Orientale, Cairo 1938, Plates 50 & 52.}} In Greco-Roman liturgy, {{ill|Seth|de|Seth|ja|セト|zh|赛特}} fled {{ill|Thoth|de|Thot|ja|トト|zh|托特}} and {{ill|Anubis|de|Anubis|ja|アヌビス|zh|阿努比斯}}, shape-shifted into a panther, and was slain by Anubis. Anubis then entered Osiris’s embalming hall wearing the flayed pelt, declaring: “Seth is here.” == See also == * {{ill|Sem-em-iritef|de|Sem-em-iritef|ja|Sem-em-iritef|zh|Sem-em-iritef}} * {{ill|Setem-atef-Usir|de|Setem-atef-Usir|ja|Setem-atef-Usir|zh|Setem-atef-Usir}} * {{ill|Min-Fest|de|Min-Fest|ja|Min-Fest|zh|Min-Fest}} * {{ill|Ancient Egyptian administrative and functional titles|de|Altägyptische Beamten- und Funktionstitel|ja|古代エジプトの行政・官職称号|zh|古埃及行政与职能头衔}} == Literature == * Friedrich-Wilhelm Bissing, Hermann Kees: ''Das Re-Heiligtum des Königs Ne-Woser-Re (Rathures), Bd. 2''. An ivory tag of [[:en:Den_(pharaoh)|King Den]] (1st Dynasty) provides the oldest iconographic evidence for the title Sem,Elisabeth Staehlin: ''Untersuchungen zur ägyptischen Tracht im Alten Reich''. Hessling, Berlin 1966, pp. 66–67, Plate XXXIV, Figure 38. then called ''Mut-nefer'' and still linked to the title {{ill|Tjet|de|Tjet|ja|トジェト|zh|杰特}}. The Sem cult held a special position—it encompassed functions of the “High Priest of {{ill|Sameref|de|Sameref (ägyptische Mythologie)|ja|サメレフ|zh|萨梅里夫}}” and underpinned worship of {{ill|Osiris-Chontamenti|de|Osiris-Chontamenti|ja|オシリス・コントァメンティ|zh|奥西里斯-孔塔门提}} at {{ill|Abydos|de|Abydos|ja|アビュドス|zh|阿比多斯}}. == Background == === Role assignment of the Sem priest === [[File:Egypt.Osiris.statuette.01.jpg|mini|140px|Statue of Osiris, Egyptian Museum (Cairo)]] The Sem priest symbolizes the caring son of Osiris in relation to {{ill|Horus|de|Horus|ja|ホルス|zh|荷鲁斯}}, providing for his deceased father. On earth, the Sem priesthood assumed tasks of the {{ill|Semu|de|Semu|ja|セム神|zh|塞姆神}} deities. As counterpart to the divine son {{ill|Sa-meref|de|Sameref|ja|サメレフ|zh|萨梅里夫}}, the Sem priest could revive the king, regenerate him, and secure his eternal rule. An inscription from the reign of {{ill|Thutmose III|de|Thutmosis III.|ja|トトメス 3世|zh|图特摩斯 三世}} by {{ill|Neb-waui|de|Neb-waui|ja|ネブワウイ|zh|内布-瓦维}} reads: {{Quote|I performed my first office in the temple of Father Osiris. I served as Sa-meref in the Golden House rites, in the mysteries of the Lord of Abydos. I was skilled in adorning the god’s statue: a Sem priest with pure fingers. All craftsmen acted under my oversight.|Neb-waui|ref=Susan Eichler Selke: ''Die Verwaltung des „Hauses des Amun“ in der 18. Dynastie''. Buske, Hamburg 2000, pp. 191–192.}} During the {{ill|18th Dynasty|de|18. Dynastie|ja|第18王朝|zh|第十八王朝}}, the {{ill|High Priest of Ptah|de|Wer-cherep-hemut|ja|プタハの大司祭|zh|普塔赫大祭司}} also bore the title Sem priest, identifying with {{ill|Iunmutef|de|Iunmutef|ja|イウンムテフ|zh|尤努穆特夫}}. Their garb gained the panther pelt and sidelock. Under {{ill|Amenhotep III|de|Amenophis III.|ja|アメンホテプ 3世|zh|阿蒙霍特普 三世}}, we see the earliest Memphite high-priest title extended with Sem status: a statue of Ptahmose (“Image of Iunmutef”) son of Mencheper. The Sem priest also adopted the “King’s Son” title of {{ill|Horus Iunmutef|de|Horus Iunmutef|ja|ホルス・イウンムテフ|zh|荷鲁斯-尤努穆特夫}}. Originally, in the {{ill|Old Kingdom|de|Altes Reich|ja|古王国|zh|古王国}}, this role was reserved for the king’s eldest son; later, the Sem priest embodied the king’s son as “Observer at the ritual purification of the deceased,” linking directly to the deity {{ill|Sem|de|Sem|ja|セム|zh|塞姆}}. === Activities of the Sem priest === The Sem priest officiated the {{ill|Opening of the Mouth ceremony|de|Mundöffnungszeremonie|ja|口開儀式|zh|开口仪式}}, depicted in panther pelt, and mediated between king and gods. He dressed the king, allocated offerings, and during {{ill|Sed festival|de|Sedfest|ja|セド祭|zh|Sed节}} processions renewed royal power. In the temple vestibule of {{ill|Pepi II|de|Pepi II.|ja|ペピ 2世|zh|佩皮 二世}}, the west wall shows the Sem offering linen, incense, and oil to the shrine gods, confirming the king as “First of the living”: {{Quote|The gods come, bringing ‘life, power, and health for the son of his body Nebmaatre’ and promise: ‘He celebrates the New Year festival with a broad heart, renews the months in the Nehebkau festival, and performs many Sed festivals on the throne like Re.’|West wall of Pepi II|ref=Gustave Jéquier: ''Le monument funéraire de Pepi II, Service des Antiquités de l'Égypte, Bd. 2: Le temple''. Institut Français d'Archéologie Orientale, Cairo 1938, Plates 50 & 52.}} In Greco-Roman liturgy, {{ill|Seth|de|Seth|ja|セト|zh|赛特}} fled {{ill|Thoth|de|Thot|ja|トト|zh|托特}} and {{ill|Anubis|de|Anubis|ja|アヌビス|zh|阿努比斯}}, shape-shifted into a panther, and was slain by Anubis. Anubis then entered Osiris’s embalming hall wearing the flayed pelt, declaring: “Seth is here.” == See also == * {{ill|Sem-em-iritef|de|Sem-em-iritef|ja|Sem-em-iritef|zh|Sem-em-iritef}} * {{ill|Setem-atef-Usir|de|Setem-atef-Usir|ja|Setem-atef-Usir|zh|Setem-atef-Usir}} * {{ill|Min-Fest|de|Min-Fest|ja|Min-Fest|zh|Min-Fest}} * {{ill|Ancient Egyptian administrative and functional titles|de|Altägyptische Beamten- und Funktionstitel|ja|古代エジプトの行政・官職称号|zh|古埃及行政与职能头衔}} == Literature == * Friedrich-Wilhelm Bissing, Hermann Kees: ''Das Re-Heiligtum des Königs Ne-Woser-Re (Rathures), Bd. 2''. Duncker, Berlin 1923, Plates 13, 18; pp. 68–69. * Hermann Kees: ''Das Priestertum im ägyptischen Staat vom Neuen Reich bis zur Spätzeit''. Brill, Leiden 1953. * Christian Leitz et al.: ''Lexikon der ägyptischen Götter und Götterbezeichnungen''. Bd. 6: “H̱ - s”. Peeters, Leuven 2002, ISBN 90-429-1151-4, p. 306. * Flinders Petrie: ''Abydos I + II''. Brian Yare Egyptology, Worcester 2005 (Reprint 1902). * Heinrich Schäfer: ''Die Mysterien des Osiris in Abydos unter König Sesostris III – Nach dem Denkstein …''. Hinrichs, Leipzig 1904, pp. 15–19. * William A. Ward: ''Index of Egyptian administrative and religious titles of the Middle Kingdom: with a glossary …''. American University of Beirut, Beirut 1982, p. 168, No. 1465. == Weblinks == * [http://www.egyptology.com/reeder/enigma/tekenu8.html The Sem priest as panther-pelt wielder] == References == {{draft categories| [[Category:WikiProject Ancient Egypt]][[Category:Priest Titles (Ancient Egypt)]][[Category:completion tier 2]] [[Category:WikiProject Ancient Egypt]][[Category:Priest Titles (Ancient Egypt)]][[Category:completion tier 2]] }} [[en::Sem priest]] [[sn:automatic wikipedia redirects]] [[sn:pages created from Interlanguage links]] [[fr:Pr%C3%AAtre-sem]] [[es:Sacerdote sem]] [[sn:Redirects to be cleared]] [[de:Sem (Priester)]][[ja:セム祭司]][[zh:瑟姆祭司]] [[Category:WikiProject Ancient Egypt]][[Category:Priest Titles (Ancient Egypt)]][[Category:completion tier 2]] [[Category:WikiProject Ancient Egypt]][[Category:Priest Titles (Ancient Egypt)]][[Category:completion tier 2]] [[Category:WikiProject Ancient Egypt]][[Category:Priest Titles (Ancient Egypt)]][[Category:completion tier 2]] [[ja:セムとムトネフェル]][[de:Sem und Mut-nefer]] {{translated page|de|Sem (Priester)|version=223454035|comment=Imported full de history, revisions duplicated and version not here due to an error with my bot related to the German being under the same pagename as the english}}

Dynastie|ja|第18王朝|zh|第十八王朝}}, the {{ill|High Priest of Ptah|de|Wer-cherep-hemut|ja|プタハの大司祭|zh|普塔赫大祭司}} also bore the title Sem priest, identifying with {{ill|Iunmutef|de|Iunmutef|ja|イウンムテフ|zh|尤努穆特夫}}. Hinrichs, Leipzig 1904, pp. 15–19. * William A. Ward: ''Index of Egyptian administrative and religious titles of the Middle Kingdom: with a glossary …''. American University of Beirut, Beirut 1982, p. 168, No. 1465. == Weblinks == * [http://www.egyptology.com/reeder/enigma/tekenu8.html The Sem priest as panther-pelt wielder] == References == {{draft categories| [[Category:WikiProject Ancient Egypt]][[Category:Priest Titles (Ancient Egypt)]][[Category:completion tier 2]] [[Category:WikiProject Ancient Egypt]][[Category:Priest Titles (Ancient Egypt)]][[Category:completion tier 2]] }} [[en::Sem priest]] [[sn:automatic wikipedia redirects]] [[sn:pages created from Interlanguage links]] [[fr:Pr%C3%AAtre-sem]] [[es:Sacerdote sem]] [[sn:Redirects to be cleared]] [[de:Sem (Priester)]][[ja:セム祭司]][[zh:瑟姆祭司]] [[Category:WikiProject Ancient Egypt]][[Category:Priest Titles (Ancient Egypt)]][[Category:completion tier 2]] [[Category:WikiProject Ancient Egypt]][[Category:Priest Titles (Ancient Egypt)]][[Category:completion tier 2]] [[Category:WikiProject Ancient Egypt]][[Category:Priest Titles (Ancient Egypt)]][[Category:completion tier 2]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Sem priest]][[Category:qq]] [[ja:セムとムトネフェル]][[de:Sem und Mut-nefer]] {{translated page|de|Sem (Priester)|version=223454035|comment=Imported full de history, revisions duplicated and version not here due to an error with my bot related to the German being under the same pagename as the english}}

revision.text:
{{Infobox hieroglyphs two-columns |TITEL = Sem and Mut-nefer |NAME = G14-F35
Mut-nefer
''Mwt-nfr'' |NAME-ERWEITERT = {{ill|Early Dynastic Period|de|Frühdynastische Periode (Ägypten)|ja|先王朝時代|zh|早期王朝時期}} |NAME2 = S29-G17
S29-X1-G17 |NAME2-ERWEITERT = {{ill|Middle Kingdom|de|Mittleres Reich|ja|中王国|zh|中王国}} |NAME3 = S29-G17-A1
S29-X1-G17-A1
Sem / Setem
''Sm / Stm'' |NAME3-ERWEITERT = {{ill|New Kingdom|de|Neues Reich|ja|新王国|zh|新王国}} |NAME4-ERWEITERT = Subtitle |NAME4 = R8-S20
{{ill|Chetemu-netjer|de|Chetemu-netjer|ja|チェテムネトジェル|zh|切特穆-内特哲}}
''Ḫtmw-nṯr''
''Seal Keeper of the God''
also: ''Divine Sigillator'' }} '''Sem''' (also '''Setem''') was, in the {{ill|Old Kingdom|de|Altes Reich|ja|古王国|zh|古王国}}, {{ill|Middle Kingdom|de|Mittleres Reich|ja|中王国|zh|中王国}}, and early {{ill|New Kingdom|de|Neues Reich|ja|新王国|zh|新王国}}, primarily the ancient Egyptian title for the “High Priest of {{ill|Osiris|de|Osiris|ja|オシリス|zh|奥西里斯}},” defined by the duties of the Sem priesthood in mortuary temples. The exact meaning of “Setem priest” in this context remains unclear. An ivory tag of [[:en:Den_(pharaoh)|King Den]] (1st Dynasty) provides the oldest iconographic evidence for the title Sem,Elisabeth Staehlin: ''Untersuchungen zur ägyptischen Tracht im Alten Reich''. Hessling, Berlin 1966, pp. 66–67, Plate XXXIV, Figure 38. then called ''Mut-nefer'' and still linked to the title {{ill|Tjet|de|Tjet|ja|トジェト|zh|杰特}}. The Sem cult held a special position—it encompassed functions of the “High Priest of {{ill|Sameref|de|Sameref (ägyptische Mythologie)|ja|サメレフ|zh|萨梅里夫}}” and underpinned worship of {{ill|Osiris-Chontamenti|de|Osiris-Chontamenti|ja|オシリス・コントァメンティ|zh|奥西里斯-孔塔门提}} at {{ill|Abydos|de|Abydos|ja|アビュドス|zh|阿比多斯}}. == Background == === Role assignment of the Sem priest === [[File:Egypt.Osiris.statuette.01.jpg|mini|140px|Statue of Osiris, Egyptian Museum (Cairo)]] The Sem priest symbolizes the caring son of Osiris in relation to {{ill|Horus|de|Horus|ja|ホルス|zh|荷鲁斯}}, providing for his deceased father. On earth, the Sem priesthood assumed tasks of the {{ill|Semu|de|Semu|ja|セム神|zh|塞姆神}} deities. As counterpart to the divine son {{ill|Sa-meref|de|Sameref|ja|サメレフ|zh|萨梅里夫}}, the Sem priest could revive the king, regenerate him, and secure his eternal rule. An inscription from the reign of {{ill|Thutmose III|de|Thutmosis III.|ja|トトメス 3世|zh|图特摩斯 三世}} by {{ill|Neb-waui|de|Neb-waui|ja|ネブワウイ|zh|内布-瓦维}} reads: {{Quote|I performed my first office in the temple of Father Osiris. I served as Sa-meref in the Golden House rites, in the mysteries of the Lord of Abydos. I was skilled in adorning the god’s statue: a Sem priest with pure fingers. All craftsmen acted under my oversight.|Neb-waui|ref=Susan Eichler Selke: ''Die Verwaltung des „Hauses des Amun“ in der 18. Dynastie''. Buske, Hamburg 2000, pp. 191–192.}} During the {{ill|18th Dynasty|de|18. Dynastie|ja|第18王朝|zh|第十八王朝}}, the {{ill|High Priest of Ptah|de|Wer-cherep-hemut|ja|プタハの大司祭|zh|普塔赫大祭司}} also bore the title Sem priest, identifying with {{ill|Iunmutef|1=Iunmutef|2=de|3=Iunmutef|4=es|5=Iunmutef|6=fr|7=Iounmoutef|8=fi|9=Inmutef|de|Iunmutef|1=Iunmutef|2=de|3=Iunmutef|4=es|5=Iunmutef|6=fr|7=Iounmoutef|8=fi|9=Inmutef|ja|イウンムテフ|zh|尤努穆特夫}}. Their garb gained the panther pelt and sidelock. Under {{ill|Amenhotep III|de|Amenophis III.|ja|アメンホテプ 3世|zh|阿蒙霍特普 三世}}, we see the earliest Memphite high-priest title extended with Sem status: a statue of Ptahmose (“Image of Iunmutef”) son of Mencheper. The Sem priest also adopted the “King’s Son” title of {{ill|Horus Iunmutef|de|Horus Iunmutef|ja|ホルス・イウンムテフ|zh|荷鲁斯-尤努穆特夫}}. Originally, in the {{ill|Old Kingdom|de|Altes Reich|ja|古王国|zh|古王国}}, this role was reserved for the king’s eldest son; later, the Sem priest embodied the king’s son as “Observer at the ritual purification of the deceased,” linking directly to the deity {{ill|Sem|de|Sem|ja|セム|zh|塞姆}}. === Activities of the Sem priest === The Sem priest officiated the {{ill|Opening of the Mouth ceremony|de|Mundöffnungsritual|1=Opening of the mouth ceremony|2=de|3=Mundöffnungsritual|4=ru|5=Отверзение уст|6=es|7=Apertura de la boca y los ojos|8=fr|9=Ouverture de la bouche|10=it|11=Cerimonia di apertura della bocca|ja|口開儀式|zh|开口仪式}}, depicted in panther pelt, and mediated between king and gods. He dressed the king, allocated offerings, and during {{ill|Sed festival|de|Sedfest|ja|セド祭|zh|Sed节}} processions renewed royal power. In the temple vestibule of {{ill|Pepi II|de|Pepi II.|ja|ペピ 2世|zh|佩皮 二世}}, the west wall shows the Sem offering linen, incense, and oil to the shrine gods, confirming the king as “First of the living”: {{Quote|The gods come, bringing ‘life, power, and health for the son of his body Nebmaatre’ and promise: ‘He celebrates the New Year festival with a broad heart, renews the months in the Nehebkau festival, and performs many Sed festivals on the throne like Re.’|West wall of Pepi II|ref=Gustave Jéquier: ''Le monument funéraire de Pepi II, Service des Antiquités de l'Égypte, Bd. 2: Le temple''. Institut Français d'Archéologie Orientale, Cairo 1938, Plates 50 & 52.}} In Greco-Roman liturgy, {{ill|Seth|de|Seth|ja|セト|zh|赛特}} fled {{ill|Thoth|de|Thot|ja|トト|zh|托特}} and {{ill|Anubis|de|Anubis|ja|アヌビス|zh|阿努比斯}}, shape-shifted into a panther, and was slain by Anubis. Anubis then entered Osiris’s embalming hall wearing the flayed pelt, declaring: “Seth is here.” == See also == * {{ill|Sem-em-iritef|de|Sem-em-iritef|ja|Sem-em-iritef|zh|Sem-em-iritef}} * {{ill|Setem-atef-Usir|de|Setem-atef-Usir|ja|Setem-atef-Usir|zh|Setem-atef-Usir}} * {{ill|Min-Fest|de|Min-Fest|ja|Min-Fest|zh|Min-Fest}} * {{ill|Ancient Egyptian administrative and functional titles|de|Altägyptische Beamten- und Funktionstitel|ja|古代エジプトの行政・官職称号|zh|古埃及行政与职能头衔}} == Literature == * Friedrich-Wilhelm Bissing, Hermann Kees: ''Das Re-Heiligtum des Königs Ne-Woser-Re (Rathures), Bd. 2''. Duncker, Berlin 1923, Plates 13, 18; pp. 68–69. * Hermann Kees: ''Das Priestertum im ägyptischen Staat vom Neuen Reich bis zur Spätzeit''. Brill, Leiden 1953. * Christian Leitz et al.: ''Lexikon der ägyptischen Götter und Götterbezeichnungen''. Bd. 6: “H̱ - s”. Peeters, Leuven 2002, ISBN 90-429-1151-4, p. 306. * Flinders Petrie: ''Abydos I + II''. Brian Yare Egyptology, Worcester 2005 (Reprint 1902). * Heinrich Schäfer: ''Die Mysterien des Osiris in Abydos unter König Sesostris III – Nach dem Denkstein …''. Hinrichs, Leipzig 1904, pp. 15–19. * William A. Ward: ''Index of Egyptian administrative and religious titles of the Middle Kingdom: with a glossary …''. American University of Beirut, Beirut 1982, p. 168, No. 1465. == Weblinks == * [http://www.egyptology.com/reeder/enigma/tekenu8.html The Sem priest as panther-pelt wielder] == References == {{draft categories| [[Category:WikiProject Ancient Egypt]][[Category:Priest Titles (Ancient Egypt)]][[Category:completion tier 2]] [[Category:WikiProject Ancient Egypt]][[Category:Priest Titles (Ancient Egypt)]][[Category:completion tier 2]] }} [[en::Sem priest]] [[sn:automatic wikipedia redirects]] [[sn:pages created from Interlanguage links]] [[fr:Pr%C3%AAtre-sem]] [[es:Sacerdote sem]] [[sn:Redirects to be cleared]] [[de:Sem (Priester)]][[ja:セム祭司]][[zh:瑟姆祭司]] [[Category:WikiProject Ancient Egypt]][[Category:Priest Titles (Ancient Egypt)]][[Category:completion tier 2]] [[Category:WikiProject Ancient Egypt]][[Category:Priest Titles (Ancient Egypt)]][[Category:completion tier 2]] [[Category:WikiProject Ancient Egypt]][[Category:Priest Titles (Ancient Egypt)]][[Category:completion tier 2]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Sem priest]][[Category:qq]] [[ja:セムとムトネフェル]][[de:Sem und Mut-nefer]] {{translated page|de|Sem (Priester)|version=223454035|comment=Imported full de history, revisions duplicated and version not here due to an error with my bot related to the German being under the same pagename as the english}}

On earth, the Sem priesthood assumed tasks of the {{ill|Semu|de|Semu|ja|セム神|zh|塞姆神}} deities. As counterpart to the divine son {{ill|Sa-meref|de|Sameref|ja|サメレフ|zh|萨梅里夫}}, the Sem priest could revive the king, regenerate him, and secure his eternal rule. An inscription from the reign of {{ill|Thutmose III|de|Thutmosis III.|ja|トトメス 3世|zh|图特摩斯 三世}} by {{ill|Neb-waui|de|Neb-waui|ja|ネブワウイ|zh|内布-瓦维}} reads: {{Quote|I performed my first office in the temple of Father Osiris. I served as Sa-meref in the Golden House rites, in the mysteries of the Lord of Abydos. I was skilled in adorning the god’s statue: a Sem priest with pure fingers. All craftsmen acted under my oversight.|Neb-waui|ref=Susan Eichler Selke: ''Die Verwaltung des „Hauses des Amun“ in der 18. Dynastie''. Buske, Hamburg 2000, pp. 191–192.}} During the {{ill|18th Dynasty|de|18. Dynastie|ja|第18王朝|zh|第十八王朝}}, the {{ill|High Priest of Ptah|de|Wer-cherep-hemut|ja|プタハの大司祭|zh|普塔赫大祭司}} also bore the title Sem priest, identifying with {{ill|Iunmutef|1=Iunmutef|2=de|3=Iunmutef|4=es|5=Iunmutef|6=fr|7=Iounmoutef|8=fi|9=Inmutef|de|Iunmutef|1=Iunmutef|2=de|3=Iunmutef|4=es|5=Iunmutef|6=fr|7=Iounmoutef|8=fi|9=Inmutef|ja|イウンムテフ|zh|尤努穆特夫}}. Their garb gained the panther pelt and sidelock. Under {{ill|Amenhotep III|de|Amenophis III.|ja|アメンホテプ 3世|zh|阿蒙霍特普 三世}}, we see the earliest Memphite high-priest title extended with Sem status: a statue of Ptahmose (“Image of Iunmutef”) son of Mencheper. The Sem priest also adopted the “King’s Son” title of {{ill|Horus Iunmutef|de|Horus Iunmutef|ja|ホルス・イウンムテフ|zh|荷鲁斯-尤努穆特夫}}. Originally, in the {{ill|Old Kingdom|de|Altes Reich|ja|古王国|zh|古王国}}, this role was reserved for the king’s eldest son; later, the Sem priest embodied the king’s son as “Observer at the ritual purification of the deceased,” linking directly to the deity {{ill|Sem|de|Sem|ja|セム|zh|塞姆}}. === Activities of the Sem priest === The Sem priest officiated the {{ill|Opening of the Mouth ceremony|de|Mundöffnungsritual|1=Opening of the mouth ceremony|2=de|3=Mundöffnungsritual|4=ru|5=Отверзение уст|6=es|7=Apertura de la boca y los ojos|8=fr|9=Ouverture de la bouche|10=it|11=Cerimonia di apertura della bocca|1=Opening of the mouth ceremony|2=de|3=Mundöffnungsritual|4=ru|5=Отверзение уст|6=es|7=Apertura de la boca y los ojos|8=fr|9=Ouverture de la bouche|10=it|11=Cerimonia di apertura della bocca|ja|口開儀式|zh|开口仪式}}, depicted in panther pelt, and mediated between king and gods. He dressed the king, allocated offerings, and during {{ill|Sed festival|de|Sedfest|ja|セド祭|zh|Sed节}} processions renewed royal power. In the temple vestibule of {{ill|Pepi II|de|Pepi II.|ja|ペピ 2世|zh|佩皮 二世}}, the west wall shows the Sem offering linen, incense, and oil to the shrine gods, confirming the king as “First of the living”: {{Quote|The gods come, bringing ‘life, power, and health for the son of his body Nebmaatre’ and promise: ‘He celebrates the New Year festival with a broad heart, renews the months in the Nehebkau festival, and performs many Sed festivals on the throne like Re.’|West wall of Pepi II|ref=Gustave Jéquier: ''Le monument funéraire de Pepi II, Service des Antiquités de l'Égypte, Bd. 2: Le temple''. Institut Français d'Archéologie Orientale, Cairo 1938, Plates 50 & 52.}} In Greco-Roman liturgy, {{ill|Seth|de|Seth|ja|セト|zh|赛特}} fled {{ill|Thoth|de|Thot|ja|トト|zh|托特}} and {{ill|Anubis|de|Anubis|ja|アヌビス|zh|阿努比斯}}, shape-shifted into a panther, and was slain by Anubis. Anubis then entered Osiris’s embalming hall wearing the flayed pelt, declaring: “Seth is here.” == See also == * {{ill|Sem-em-iritef|de|Sem-em-iritef|ja|Sem-em-iritef|zh|Sem-em-iritef}} * {{ill|Setem-atef-Usir|de|Setem-atef-Usir|ja|Setem-atef-Usir|zh|Setem-atef-Usir}} * {{ill|Min-Fest|de|Min-Fest|ja|Min-Fest|zh|Min-Fest}} * {{ill|Ancient Egyptian administrative and functional titles|de|Altägyptische Beamten- und Funktionstitel|ja|古代エジプトの行政・官職称号|zh|古埃及行政与职能头衔}} == Literature == * Friedrich-Wilhelm Bissing, Hermann Kees: ''Das Re-Heiligtum des Königs Ne-Woser-Re (Rathures), Bd. 2''. Duncker, Berlin 1923, Plates 13, 18; pp. 68–69. * Hermann Kees: ''Das Priestertum im ägyptischen Staat vom Neuen Reich bis zur Spätzeit''. Brill, Leiden 1953. * Christian Leitz et al.: ''Lexikon der ägyptischen Götter und Götterbezeichnungen''. Bd. 6: “H̱ - s”. Peeters, Leuven 2002, ISBN 90-429-1151-4, p. 306. * Flinders Petrie: ''Abydos I + II''. Brian Yare Egyptology, Worcester 2005 (Reprint 1902). * Heinrich Schäfer: ''Die Mysterien des Osiris in Abydos unter König Sesostris III – Nach dem Denkstein …''. Hinrichs, Leipzig 1904, pp. 15–19. * William A. Ward: ''Index of Egyptian administrative and religious titles of the Middle Kingdom: with a glossary …''. American University of Beirut, Beirut 1982, p. 168, No. 1465. == Weblinks == * [http://www.egyptology.com/reeder/enigma/tekenu8.html The Sem priest as panther-pelt wielder] == References == {{draft categories| [[Category:WikiProject Ancient Egypt]][[Category:Priest Titles (Ancient Egypt)]][[Category:completion tier 2]] [[Category:WikiProject Ancient Egypt]][[Category:Priest Titles (Ancient Egypt)]][[Category:completion tier 2]] }} [[en::Sem priest]] [[sn:automatic wikipedia redirects]] [[sn:pages created from Interlanguage links]] [[fr:Pr%C3%AAtre-sem]] [[es:Sacerdote sem]] [[sn:Redirects to be cleared]] [[de:Sem (Priester)]][[ja:セム祭司]][[zh:瑟姆祭司]] [[Category:WikiProject Ancient Egypt]][[Category:Priest Titles (Ancient Egypt)]][[Category:completion tier 2]] [[Category:WikiProject Ancient Egypt]][[Category:Priest Titles (Ancient Egypt)]][[Category:completion tier 2]] [[Category:WikiProject Ancient Egypt]][[Category:Priest Titles (Ancient Egypt)]][[Category:completion tier 2]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Sem priest]][[Category:qq]] [[ja:セムとムトネフェル]][[de:Sem und Mut-nefer]] {{translated page|de|Sem (Priester)|version=223454035|comment=Imported full de history, revisions duplicated and version not here due to an error with my bot related to the German being under the same pagename as the english}}

''Ḫtmw-nṯr''
''Seal Keeper of the God''
also: ''Divine Sigillator'' }} '''Sem''' (also '''Setem''') was, in the [[:en:Old Kingdom of Egypt|Old Kingdom]], [[:en:Middle Kingdom of Egypt|Middle Kingdom]], and early [[:en:New Kingdom of Egypt|New Kingdom]], primarily the ancient Egyptian title for the “High Priest of [[:en:Osiris|Osiris]],” defined by the duties of the Sem priesthood in mortuary temples. The exact meaning of “Setem priest” in this context remains unclear. An ivory tag of [[:en:Den_(pharaoh)|King Den]] (1st Dynasty) provides the oldest iconographic evidence for the title Sem,Elisabeth Staehlin: ''Untersuchungen zur ägyptischen Tracht im Alten Reich''. Hessling, Berlin 1966, pp. 66–67, Plate XXXIV, Figure 38. then called ''Mut-nefer'' and still linked to the title {{ill|Tjet|de|Tjet|ja|トジェト|zh|杰特}}. The Sem cult held a special position—it encompassed functions of the “High Priest of {{ill|Sameref|de|Sameref (ägyptische Mythologie)|ja|サメレフ|zh|萨梅里夫}}” and underpinned worship of {{ill|Osiris-Chontamenti|de|Osiris-Chontamenti|ja|オシリス・コントァメンティ|zh|奥西里斯-孔塔门提}} at [[:en:Abydos|Abydos]]. == Background == === Role assignment of the Sem priest === [[File:Egypt.Osiris.statuette.01.jpg|mini|140px|Statue of Osiris, Egyptian Museum (Cairo)]] The Sem priest symbolizes the caring son of Osiris in relation to [[:en:Horus|Horus]], providing for his deceased father. On earth, the Sem priesthood assumed tasks of the {{ill|Semu|de|Semu|ja|セム神|zh|塞姆神}} deities. As counterpart to the divine son {{ill|Sa-meref|de|Sameref|ja|サメレフ|zh|萨梅里夫}}, the Sem priest could revive the king, regenerate him, and secure his eternal rule. An inscription from the reign of [[:en:Thutmose III|Thutmose III]] by {{ill|Neb-waui|de|Neb-waui|ja|ネブワウイ|zh|内布-瓦维}} reads: {{Quote|I performed my first office in the temple of Father Osiris. I served as Sa-meref in the Golden House rites, in the mysteries of the Lord of Abydos. I was skilled in adorning the god’s statue: a Sem priest with pure fingers. All craftsmen acted under my oversight.|Neb-waui|ref=Susan Eichler Selke: ''Die Verwaltung des „Hauses des Amun“ in der 18. Dynastie''. Buske, Hamburg 2000, pp. 191–192.}} During the [[:en:Eighteenth Dynasty of Egypt|18th Dynasty]], the {{ill|High Priest of Ptah|de|Wer-cherep-hemut|ja|プタハの大司祭|zh|普塔赫大祭司}} also bore the title Sem priest, identifying with {{ill|Iunmutef|1=Iunmutef|2=de|3=Iunmutef|4=es|5=Iunmutef|6=fr|7=Iounmoutef|8=fi|9=Inmutef|de|Iunmutef|1=Iunmutef|2=de|3=Iunmutef|4=es|5=Iunmutef|6=fr|7=Iounmoutef|8=fi|9=Inmutef|ja|イウンムテフ|zh|尤努穆特夫}}. Their garb gained the panther pelt and sidelock. Under [[:en:Amenhotep III|Amenhotep III]], we see the earliest Memphite high-priest title extended with Sem status: a statue of Ptahmose (“Image of Iunmutef”) son of Mencheper. The Sem priest also adopted the “King’s Son” title of {{ill|Horus Iunmutef|de|Horus Iunmutef|ja|ホルス・イウンムテフ|zh|荷鲁斯-尤努穆特夫}}. Originally, in the [[:en:Old Kingdom of Egypt|Old Kingdom]], this role was reserved for the king’s eldest son; later, the Sem priest embodied the king’s son as “Observer at the ritual purification of the deceased,” linking directly to the deity [[:en:SEM|Sem]]. === Activities of the Sem priest === The Sem priest officiated the [[:en:Opening of the mouth ceremony|Opening of the mouth ceremony]], depicted in panther pelt, and mediated between king and gods. He dressed the king, allocated offerings, and during [[:en:Sed festival|Sed festival]] processions renewed royal power. In the temple vestibule of [[:en:Pepi II Neferkare|Pepi II]], the west wall shows the Sem offering linen, incense, and oil to the shrine gods, confirming the king as “First of the living”: {{Quote|The gods come, bringing ‘life, power, and health for the son of his body Nebmaatre’ and promise: ‘He celebrates the New Year festival with a broad heart, renews the months in the Nehebkau festival, and performs many Sed festivals on the throne like Re.’|West wall of Pepi II|ref=Gustave Jéquier: ''Le monument funéraire de Pepi II, Service des Antiquités de l'Égypte, Bd. 2: Le temple''. Institut Français d'Archéologie Orientale, Cairo 1938, Plates 50 & 52.}} In Greco-Roman liturgy, [[:en:Seth (disambiguation)|Seth]] fled [[:en:Thoth|Thoth]] and [[:en:Anubis|Anubis]], shape-shifted into a panther, and was slain by Anubis. Anubis then entered Osiris’s embalming hall wearing the flayed pelt, declaring: “Seth is here.” == See also == * {{ill|Sem-em-iritef|de|Sem-em-iritef|ja|Sem-em-iritef|zh|Sem-em-iritef}} * {{ill|Setem-atef-Usir|de|Setem-atef-Usir|ja|Setem-atef-Usir|zh|Setem-atef-Usir}} * [[:en:Min festival|Min-Fest]] * {{ill|Ancient Egyptian administrative and functional titles|de|Altägyptische Beamten- und Funktionstitel|ja|古代エジプトの行政・官職称号|zh|古埃及行政与职能头衔}} == Literature == * Friedrich-Wilhelm Bissing, Hermann Kees: ''Das Re-Heiligtum des Königs Ne-Woser-Re (Rathures), Bd. 2''. Duncker, Berlin 1923, Plates 13, 18; pp. 68–69. * Hermann Kees: ''Das Priestertum im ägyptischen Staat vom Neuen Reich bis zur Spätzeit''. Brill, Leiden 1953. * Christian Leitz et al.: ''Lexikon der ägyptischen Götter und Götterbezeichnungen''. Bd. 6: “H̱ - s”. Peeters, Leuven 2002, ISBN 90-429-1151-4, p. 306. * Flinders Petrie: ''Abydos I + II''. Brian Yare Egyptology, Worcester 2005 (Reprint 1902). * Heinrich Schäfer: ''Die Mysterien des Osiris in Abydos unter König Sesostris III – Nach dem Denkstein …''. Hinrichs, Leipzig 1904, pp. 15–19. * William A. Ward: ''Index of Egyptian administrative and religious titles of the Middle Kingdom: with a glossary …''. American University of Beirut, Beirut 1982, p. 168, No. 1465. == Weblinks == * [http://www.egyptology.com/reeder/enigma/tekenu8.html The Sem priest as panther-pelt wielder] == References == {{draft categories| [[Category:WikiProject Ancient Egypt]][[Category:Priest Titles (Ancient Egypt)]][[Category:completion tier 2]] [[Category:WikiProject Ancient Egypt]][[Category:Priest Titles (Ancient Egypt)]][[Category:completion tier 2]] }} [[en::Sem priest]] [[sn:automatic wikipedia redirects]] [[sn:pages created from Interlanguage links]] [[fr:Pr%C3%AAtre-sem]] [[es:Sacerdote sem]] [[sn:Redirects to be cleared]] [[de:Sem (Priester)]][[ja:セム祭司]][[zh:瑟姆祭司]] [[Category:WikiProject Ancient Egypt]][[Category:Priest Titles (Ancient Egypt)]][[Category:completion tier 2]] [[Category:WikiProject Ancient Egypt]][[Category:Priest Titles (Ancient Egypt)]][[Category:completion tier 2]] [[Category:WikiProject Ancient Egypt]][[Category:Priest Titles (Ancient Egypt)]][[Category:completion tier 2]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Sem priest]][[Category:qq]] [[ja:セムとムトネフェル]][[de:Sem und Mut-nefer]] {{translated page|de|Sem (Priester)|version=223454035|comment=Imported full de history, revisions duplicated and version not here due to an error with my bot related to the German being under the same pagename as the english}}

The exact meaning of “Setem priest” in this context remains unclear. An ivory tag of [[:en:Den_(pharaoh)|King Den]] (1st Dynasty) provides the oldest iconographic evidence for the title Sem,Elisabeth Staehlin: ''Untersuchungen zur ägyptischen Tracht im Alten Reich''. Hessling, Berlin 1966, pp. 66–67, Plate XXXIV, Figure 38. then called ''Mut-nefer'' and still linked to the title {{ill|Tjet|de|Tjet|ja|トジェト|zh|杰特}}. The Sem cult held a special position—it encompassed functions of the “High Priest of {{ill|Sameref|de|Sameref (ägyptische Mythologie)|ja|サメレフ|zh|萨梅里夫}}” and underpinned worship of {{ill|Osiris-Chontamenti|de|Osiris-Chontamenti|ja|オシリス・コントァメンティ|zh|奥西里斯-孔塔门提}} at [[:en:Abydos|Abydos]]. == Background == === Role assignment of the Sem priest === [[File:Egypt.Osiris.statuette.01.jpg|thumb|140px|Statue of Osiris, Egyptian Museum (Cairo)]] The Sem priest symbolizes the caring son of Osiris in relation to [[:en:Horus|Horus]], providing for his deceased father. On earth, the Sem priesthood assumed tasks of the {{ill|Semu|de|Semu|ja|セム神|zh|塞姆神}} deities. As counterpart to the divine son {{ill|Sa-meref|de|Sameref|ja|サメレフ|zh|萨梅里夫}}, the Sem priest could revive the king, regenerate him, and secure his eternal rule. An inscription from the reign of [[:en:Thutmose III|Thutmose III]] by {{ill|Neb-waui|de|Neb-waui|ja|ネブワウイ|zh|内布-瓦维}} reads: {{Quote|I performed my first office in the temple of Father Osiris. I served as Sa-meref in the Golden House rites, in the mysteries of the Lord of Abydos. I was skilled in adorning the god’s statue: a Sem priest with pure fingers. All craftsmen acted under my oversight.|Neb-waui|ref=Susan Eichler Selke: ''Die Verwaltung des „Hauses des Amun“ in der 18. Dynastie''. Buske, Hamburg 2000, pp. 191–192.}} During the [[:en:Eighteenth Dynasty of Egypt|18th Dynasty]], the {{ill|High Priest of Ptah|de|Wer-cherep-hemut|ja|プタハの大司祭|zh|普塔赫大祭司}} also bore the title Sem priest, identifying with {{ill|Iunmutef|1=Iunmutef|2=de|3=Iunmutef|4=es|5=Iunmutef|6=fr|7=Iounmoutef|8=fi|9=Inmutef|de|Iunmutef|1=Iunmutef|2=de|3=Iunmutef|4=es|5=Iunmutef|6=fr|7=Iounmoutef|8=fi|9=Inmutef|ja|イウンムテフ|zh|尤努穆特夫}}. Their garb gained the panther pelt and sidelock. Under [[:en:Amenhotep III|Amenhotep III]], we see the earliest Memphite high-priest title extended with Sem status: a statue of Ptahmose (“Image of Iunmutef”) son of Mencheper. The Sem priest also adopted the “King’s Son” title of {{ill|Horus Iunmutef|de|Horus Iunmutef|ja|ホルス・イウンムテフ|zh|荷鲁斯-尤努穆特夫}}. Originally, in the [[:en:Old Kingdom of Egypt|Old Kingdom]], this role was reserved for the king’s eldest son; later, the Sem priest embodied the king’s son as “Observer at the ritual purification of the deceased,” linking directly to the deity [[:en:SEM|Sem]]. === Activities of the Sem priest === The Sem priest officiated the [[:en:Opening of the mouth ceremony|Opening of the mouth ceremony]], depicted in panther pelt, and mediated between king and gods. He dressed the king, allocated offerings, and during [[:en:Sed festival|Sed festival]] processions renewed royal power. In the temple vestibule of [[:en:Pepi II Neferkare|Pepi II]], the west wall shows the Sem offering linen, incense, and oil to the shrine gods, confirming the king as “First of the living”: {{Quote|The gods come, bringing ‘life, power, and health for the son of his body Nebmaatre’ and promise: ‘He celebrates the New Year festival with a broad heart, renews the months in the Nehebkau festival, and performs many Sed festivals on the throne like Re.’|West wall of Pepi II|ref=Gustave Jéquier: ''Le monument funéraire de Pepi II, Service des Antiquités de l'Égypte, Bd. 2: Le temple''. Institut Français d'Archéologie Orientale, Cairo 1938, Plates 50 & 52.}} In Greco-Roman liturgy, [[:en:Seth (disambiguation)|Seth]] fled [[:en:Thoth|Thoth]] and [[:en:Anubis|Anubis]], shape-shifted into a panther, and was slain by Anubis. Anubis then entered Osiris’s embalming hall wearing the flayed pelt, declaring: “Seth is here.” == See also == * {{ill|Sem-em-iritef|de|Sem-em-iritef|ja|Sem-em-iritef|zh|Sem-em-iritef}} * {{ill|Setem-atef-Usir|de|Setem-atef-Usir|ja|Setem-atef-Usir|zh|Setem-atef-Usir}} * [[:en:Min festival|Min-Fest]] * {{ill|Ancient Egyptian administrative and functional titles|de|Altägyptische Beamten- und Funktionstitel|ja|古代エジプトの行政・官職称号|zh|古埃及行政与职能头衔}} == Literature == * Friedrich-Wilhelm Bissing, Hermann Kees: ''Das Re-Heiligtum des Königs Ne-Woser-Re (Rathures), Bd. 2''. Duncker, Berlin 1923, Plates 13, 18; pp. 68–69. * Hermann Kees: ''Das Priestertum im ägyptischen Staat vom Neuen Reich bis zur Spätzeit''. Brill, Leiden 1953. * Christian Leitz et al.: ''Lexikon der ägyptischen Götter und Götterbezeichnungen''. Bd. 6: “H̱ - s”. Peeters, Leuven 2002, ISBN 90-429-1151-4, p. 306. * Flinders Petrie: ''Abydos I + II''. Brian Yare Egyptology, Worcester 2005 (Reprint 1902). * Heinrich Schäfer: ''Die Mysterien des Osiris in Abydos unter König Sesostris III – Nach dem Denkstein …''. Hinrichs, Leipzig 1904, pp. 15–19. * William A. Ward: ''Index of Egyptian administrative and religious titles of the Middle Kingdom: with a glossary …''. American University of Beirut, Beirut 1982, p. 168, No. 1465. == Weblinks == * [http://www.egyptology.com/reeder/enigma/tekenu8.html The Sem priest as panther-pelt wielder] == References == {{draft categories| [[Category:WikiProject Ancient Egypt]][[Category:Priest Titles (Ancient Egypt)]][[Category:completion tier 2]] [[Category:WikiProject Ancient Egypt]][[Category:Priest Titles (Ancient Egypt)]][[Category:completion tier 2]] }} [[en::Sem priest]] [[sn:automatic wikipedia redirects]] [[sn:pages created from Interlanguage links]] [[fr:Pr%C3%AAtre-sem]] [[es:Sacerdote sem]] [[sn:Redirects to be cleared]] [[de:Sem (Priester)]][[ja:セム祭司]][[zh:瑟姆祭司]] [[Category:WikiProject Ancient Egypt]][[Category:Priest Titles (Ancient Egypt)]][[Category:completion tier 2]] [[Category:WikiProject Ancient Egypt]][[Category:Priest Titles (Ancient Egypt)]][[Category:completion tier 2]] [[Category:WikiProject Ancient Egypt]][[Category:Priest Titles (Ancient Egypt)]][[Category:completion tier 2]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Sem priest]][[Category:qq]] [[ja:セムとムトネフェル]][[de:Sem und Mut-nefer]] {{translated page|de|Sem (Priester)|version=223454035|comment=Imported full de history, revisions duplicated and version not here due to an error with my bot related to the German being under the same pagename as the english}} [[Category:Priestertitel (Altes Ägypten)]]

An inscription from the reign of [[:en:Thutmose III|Thutmose III]] by {{ill|Neb-waui|de|Neb-waui|ja|ネブワウイ|zh|内布-瓦维}} reads: {{Quote|I performed my first office in the temple of Father Osiris. I served as Sa-meref in the Golden House rites, in the mysteries of the Lord of Abydos. I was skilled in adorning the god’s statue: a Sem priest with pure fingers. The exact meaning of “Setem priest” in this context remains unclear. An ivory tag of [[:en:Den_(pharaoh)|King Den]] (1st Dynasty) provides the oldest iconographic evidence for the title Sem,Elisabeth Staehlin: ''Untersuchungen zur ägyptischen Tracht im Alten Reich''. Hessling, Berlin 1966, pp. 66–67, Plate XXXIV, Figure 38. then called ''Mut-nefer'' and still linked to the title {{ill|Tjet|de|Tjet|ja|トジェト|zh|杰特|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}. The Sem cult held a special position—it encompassed functions of the “High Priest of {{ill|Sameref|de|Sameref (ägyptische Mythologie)|ja|サメレフ|zh|萨梅里夫|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}” and underpinned worship of {{ill|Osiris-Chontamenti|de|Osiris-Chontamenti|ja|オシリス・コントァメンティ|zh|奥西里斯-孔塔门提|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} at [[:en:Abydos|Abydos]]. == Background == === Role assignment of the Sem priest === [[File:Egypt.Osiris.statuette.01.jpg|thumb|140px|Statue of Osiris, Egyptian Museum (Cairo)]] The Sem priest symbolizes the caring son of Osiris in relation to [[:en:Horus|Horus]], providing for his deceased father. On earth, the Sem priesthood assumed tasks of the {{ill|Semu|de|Semu|ja|セム神|zh|塞姆神|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} deities. As counterpart to the divine son {{ill|Sa-meref|de|Sameref|ja|サメレフ|zh|萨梅里夫|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, the Sem priest could revive the king, regenerate him, and secure his eternal rule. An inscription from the reign of [[:en:Thutmose III|Thutmose III]] by {{ill|Neb-waui|de|Neb-waui|ja|ネブワウイ|zh|内布-瓦维|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} reads: {{Quote|I performed my first office in the temple of Father Osiris. I served as Sa-meref in the Golden House rites, in the mysteries of the Lord of Abydos. I was skilled in adorning the god’s statue: a Sem priest with pure fingers. All craftsmen acted under my oversight.|Neb-waui|ref=Susan Eichler Selke: ''Die Verwaltung des „Hauses des Amun“ in der 18. Dynastie''. Buske, Hamburg 2000, pp. 191–192.}} During the [[:en:Eighteenth Dynasty of Egypt|18th Dynasty]], the {{ill|High Priest of Ptah|de|Wer-cherep-hemut|ja|プタハの大司祭|zh|普塔赫大祭司|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} also bore the title Sem priest, identifying with {{ill|Iunmutef|1=Iunmutef|2=de|3=Iunmutef|4=es|5=Iunmutef|6=fr|7=Iounmoutef|8=fi|9=Inmutef|de|Iunmutef|1=Iunmutef|2=de|3=Iunmutef|4=es|5=Iunmutef|6=fr|7=Iounmoutef|8=fi|9=Inmutef|ja|イウンムテフ|zh|尤努穆特夫|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}. Their garb gained the panther pelt and sidelock. Under [[:en:Amenhotep III|Amenhotep III]], we see the earliest Memphite high-priest title extended with Sem status: a statue of Ptahmose (“Image of Iunmutef”) son of Mencheper. The Sem priest also adopted the “King’s Son” title of {{ill|Horus Iunmutef|de|Horus Iunmutef|ja|ホルス・イウンムテフ|zh|荷鲁斯-尤努穆特夫|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}. Originally, in the [[:en:Old Kingdom of Egypt|Old Kingdom]], this role was reserved for the king’s eldest son; later, the Sem priest embodied the king’s son as “Observer at the ritual purification of the deceased,” linking directly to the deity [[:en:SEM|Sem]]. === Activities of the Sem priest === The Sem priest officiated the [[:en:Opening of the mouth ceremony|Opening of the mouth ceremony]], depicted in panther pelt, and mediated between king and gods. He dressed the king, allocated offerings, and during [[:en:Sed festival|Sed festival]] processions renewed royal power. In the temple vestibule of [[:en:Pepi II Neferkare|Pepi II]], the west wall shows the Sem offering linen, incense, and oil to the shrine gods, confirming the king as “First of the living”: {{Quote|The gods come, bringing ‘life, power, and health for the son of his body Nebmaatre’ and promise: ‘He celebrates the New Year festival with a broad heart, renews the months in the Nehebkau festival, and performs many Sed festivals on the throne like Re.’|West wall of Pepi II|ref=Gustave Jéquier: ''Le monument funéraire de Pepi II, Service des Antiquités de l'Égypte, Bd. 2: Le temple''. Institut Français d'Archéologie Orientale, Cairo 1938, Plates 50 & 52.}} In Greco-Roman liturgy, [[:en:Seth (disambiguation)|Seth]] fled [[:en:Thoth|Thoth]] and [[:en:Anubis|Anubis]], shape-shifted into a panther, and was slain by Anubis. Anubis then entered Osiris’s embalming hall wearing the flayed pelt, declaring: “Seth is here.” == See also == * {{ill|Sem-em-iritef|de|Sem-em-iritef|ja|Sem-em-iritef|zh|Sem-em-iritef|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} * {{ill|Setem-atef-Usir|de|Setem-atef-Usir|ja|Setem-atef-Usir|zh|Setem-atef-Usir|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} * [[:en:Min festival|Min-Fest]] * {{ill|Ancient Egyptian administrative and functional titles|de|Altägyptische Beamten- und Funktionstitel|ja|古代エジプトの行政・官職称号|zh|古埃及行政与职能头衔|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} == Literature == * Friedrich-Wilhelm Bissing, Hermann Kees: ''Das Re-Heiligtum des Königs Ne-Woser-Re (Rathures), Bd. 2''. Duncker, Berlin 1923, Plates 13, 18; pp. 68–69. * Hermann Kees: ''Das Priestertum im ägyptischen Staat vom Neuen Reich bis zur Spätzeit''. Brill, Leiden 1953. * Christian Leitz et al.: ''Lexikon der ägyptischen Götter und Götterbezeichnungen''. Bd. 6: “H̱ - s”. Peeters, Leuven 2002, ISBN 90-429-1151-4, p. 306. * Flinders Petrie: ''Abydos I + II''. Brian Yare Egyptology, Worcester 2005 (Reprint 1902). * Heinrich Schäfer: ''Die Mysterien des Osiris in Abydos unter König Sesostris III – Nach dem Denkstein …''. Hinrichs, Leipzig 1904, pp. 15–19. * William A. Ward: ''Index of Egyptian administrative and religious titles of the Middle Kingdom: with a glossary …''. American University of Beirut, Beirut 1982, p. 168, No. 1465. == Weblinks == * [http://www.egyptology.com/reeder/enigma/tekenu8.html The Sem priest as panther-pelt wielder] == References == {{draft categories| [[Category:WikiProject Ancient Egypt]][[Category:Priest Titles (Ancient Egypt)]][[Category:completion tier 2]] [[Category:WikiProject Ancient Egypt]][[Category:Priest Titles (Ancient Egypt)]][[Category:completion tier 2]] }} [[sn:automatic wikipedia redirects]] [[sn:pages created from Interlanguage links]] [[fr:Pr%C3%AAtre-sem]] [[es:Sacerdote sem]] [[sn:Redirects to be cleared]] [[de:Sem (Priester)]][[ja:セム祭司]][[zh:瑟姆祭司]] [[Category:WikiProject Ancient Egypt]][[Category:Priest Titles (Ancient Egypt)]][[Category:completion tier 2]] [[Category:WikiProject Ancient Egypt]][[Category:Priest Titles (Ancient Egypt)]][[Category:completion tier 2]] [[Category:WikiProject Ancient Egypt]][[Category:Priest Titles (Ancient Egypt)]][[Category:completion tier 2]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Sem priest]][[Category:qq]] [[ja:セムとムトネフェル]][[de:Sem und Mut-nefer]] {{translated page|de|Sem (Priester)|version=223454035|comment=Imported full de history, revisions duplicated and version not here due to an error with my bot related to the German being under the same pagename as the english}} [[Category:Priestertitel (Altes Ägypten)]] [[fr:Prêtre-sem]]

{{afc comment|Wikidata entry (none) Wikidata name: (none) [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:10, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Infobox hieroglyphs two-columns |TITEL = Sem and Mut-nefer |NAME = G14-F35
Mut-nefer
''Mwt-nfr'' |NAME-ERWEITERT = [[:en:Early Dynastic Period (Egypt)|Early Dynastic Period]] |NAME2 = S29-G17
S29-X1-G17 |NAME2-ERWEITERT = [[:en:Middle Kingdom of Egypt|Middle Kingdom]] |NAME3 = S29-G17-A1
S29-X1-G17-A1
Sem / Setem
''Sm / Stm'' |NAME3-ERWEITERT = [[:en:New Kingdom of Egypt|New Kingdom]] |NAME4-ERWEITERT = Subtitle |NAME4 = R8-S20
{{ill|Chetemu-netjer|de|Chetemu-netjer|ja|チェテムネトジェル|zh|切特穆-内特哲|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}
''Ḫtmw-nṯr''
''Seal Keeper of the God''
also: ''Divine Sigillator'' }} '''Sem''' (also '''Setem''') was, in the [[:en:Old Kingdom of Egypt|Old Kingdom]], [[:en:Middle Kingdom of Egypt|Middle Kingdom]], and early [[:en:New Kingdom of Egypt|New Kingdom]], primarily the ancient Egyptian title for the “High Priest of [[:en:Osiris|Osiris]],” defined by the duties of the Sem priesthood in mortuary temples. The exact meaning of “Setem priest” in this context remains unclear. An ivory tag of [[:en:Den_(pharaoh)|King Den]] (1st Dynasty) provides the oldest iconographic evidence for the title Sem,Elisabeth Staehlin: ''Untersuchungen zur ägyptischen Tracht im Alten Reich''. Hessling, Berlin 1966, pp. 66–67, Plate XXXIV, Figure 38. then called ''Mut-nefer'' and still linked to the title {{ill|Tjet|de|Tjet|ja|トジェト|zh|杰特|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}. The Sem cult held a special position—it encompassed functions of the “High Priest of {{ill|Sameref|de|Sameref (ägyptische Mythologie)|ja|サメレフ|zh|萨梅里夫|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}” and underpinned worship of {{ill|Osiris-Chontamenti|de|Osiris-Chontamenti|ja|オシリス・コントァメンティ|zh|奥西里斯-孔塔门提|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} at [[:en:Abydos|Abydos]]. == Background == === Role assignment of the Sem priest === [[File:Egypt.Osiris.statuette.01.jpg|thumb|140px|Statue of Osiris, Egyptian Museum (Cairo)]] The Sem priest symbolizes the caring son of Osiris in relation to [[:en:Horus|Horus]], providing for his deceased father. On earth, the Sem priesthood assumed tasks of the {{ill|Semu|de|Semu|ja|セム神|zh|塞姆神|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} deities. As counterpart to the divine son {{ill|Sa-meref|de|Sameref|ja|サメレフ|zh|萨梅里夫|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, the Sem priest could revive the king, regenerate him, and secure his eternal rule. An inscription from the reign of [[:en:Thutmose III|Thutmose III]] by {{ill|Neb-waui|de|Neb-waui|ja|ネブワウイ|zh|内布-瓦维|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} reads: {{Quote|I performed my first office in the temple of Father Osiris. I served as Sa-meref in the Golden House rites, in the mysteries of the Lord of Abydos. I was skilled in adorning the god’s statue: a Sem priest with pure fingers. All craftsmen acted under my oversight.|Neb-waui|ref=Susan Eichler Selke: ''Die Verwaltung des „Hauses des Amun“ in der 18. Dynastie''. Buske, Hamburg 2000, pp. 191–192.}} During the [[:en:Eighteenth Dynasty of Egypt|18th Dynasty]], the {{ill|High Priest of Ptah|de|Wer-cherep-hemut|ja|プタハの大司祭|zh|普塔赫大祭司|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} also bore the title Sem priest, identifying with {{ill|Iunmutef|1=Iunmutef|2=de|3=Iunmutef|4=es|5=Iunmutef|6=fr|7=Iounmoutef|8=fi|9=Inmutef|de|Iunmutef|1=Iunmutef|2=de|3=Iunmutef|4=es|5=Iunmutef|6=fr|7=Iounmoutef|8=fi|9=Inmutef|ja|イウンムテフ|zh|尤努穆特夫|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}. Their garb gained the panther pelt and sidelock. Under [[:en:Amenhotep III|Amenhotep III]], we see the earliest Memphite high-priest title extended with Sem status: a statue of Ptahmose (“Image of Iunmutef”) son of Mencheper. The Sem priest also adopted the “King’s Son” title of {{ill|Horus Iunmutef|de|Horus Iunmutef|ja|ホルス・イウンムテフ|zh|荷鲁斯-尤努穆特夫|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}. Originally, in the [[:en:Old Kingdom of Egypt|Old Kingdom]], this role was reserved for the king’s eldest son; later, the Sem priest embodied the king’s son as “Observer at the ritual purification of the deceased,” linking directly to the deity [[:en:SEM|Sem]]. === Activities of the Sem priest === The Sem priest officiated the [[:en:Opening of the mouth ceremony|Opening of the mouth ceremony]], depicted in panther pelt, and mediated between king and gods. He dressed the king, allocated offerings, and during [[:en:Sed festival|Sed festival]] processions renewed royal power. In the temple vestibule of [[:en:Pepi II Neferkare|Pepi II]], the west wall shows the Sem offering linen, incense, and oil to the shrine gods, confirming the king as “First of the living”: {{Quote|The gods come, bringing ‘life, power, and health for the son of his body Nebmaatre’ and promise: ‘He celebrates the New Year festival with a broad heart, renews the months in the Nehebkau festival, and performs many Sed festivals on the throne like Re.’|West wall of Pepi II|ref=Gustave Jéquier: ''Le monument funéraire de Pepi II, Service des Antiquités de l'Égypte, Bd. 2: Le temple''. Institut Français d'Archéologie Orientale, Cairo 1938, Plates 50 & 52.}} In Greco-Roman liturgy, [[:en:Seth (disambiguation)|Seth]] fled [[:en:Thoth|Thoth]] and [[:en:Anubis|Anubis]], shape-shifted into a panther, and was slain by Anubis. Anubis then entered Osiris’s embalming hall wearing the flayed pelt, declaring: “Seth is here.” == See also == * {{ill|Sem-em-iritef|de|Sem-em-iritef|ja|Sem-em-iritef|zh|Sem-em-iritef|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} * {{ill|Setem-atef-Usir|de|Setem-atef-Usir|ja|Setem-atef-Usir|zh|Setem-atef-Usir|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} * [[:en:Min festival|Min-Fest]] * {{ill|Ancient Egyptian administrative and functional titles|de|Altägyptische Beamten- und Funktionstitel|ja|古代エジプトの行政・官職称号|zh|古埃及行政与职能头衔|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} == Literature == * Friedrich-Wilhelm Bissing, Hermann Kees: ''Das Re-Heiligtum des Königs Ne-Woser-Re (Rathures), Bd. 2''. Duncker, Berlin 1923, Plates 13, 18; pp. 68–69. * Hermann Kees: ''Das Priestertum im ägyptischen Staat vom Neuen Reich bis zur Spätzeit''. Brill, Leiden 1953. * Christian Leitz et al.: ''Lexikon der ägyptischen Götter und Götterbezeichnungen''. Bd. 6: “H̱ - s”. Peeters, Leuven 2002, ISBN 90-429-1151-4, p. 306. * Flinders Petrie: ''Abydos I + II''. Brian Yare Egyptology, Worcester 2005 (Reprint 1902). * Heinrich Schäfer: ''Die Mysterien des Osiris in Abydos unter König Sesostris III – Nach dem Denkstein …''. Hinrichs, Leipzig 1904, pp. 15–19. * William A. Ward: ''Index of Egyptian administrative and religious titles of the Middle Kingdom: with a glossary …''. American University of Beirut, Beirut 1982, p. 168, No. 1465. == Weblinks == * [http://www.egyptology.com/reeder/enigma/tekenu8.html The Sem priest as panther-pelt wielder] == References == {{draft categories| [[Category:WikiProject Ancient Egypt]][[Category:Priest Titles (Ancient Egypt)]][[Category:completion tier 2]] [[Category:WikiProject Ancient Egypt]][[Category:Priest Titles (Ancient Egypt)]][[Category:completion tier 2]] }} [[sn:automatic wikipedia redirects]] [[sn:pages created from Interlanguage links]] [[fr:Pr%C3%AAtre-sem]] [[es:Sacerdote sem]] [[sn:Redirects to be cleared]] [[de:Sem (Priester)]][[ja:セム祭司]][[zh:瑟姆祭司]] [[Category:WikiProject Ancient Egypt]][[Category:Priest Titles (Ancient Egypt)]][[Category:completion tier 2]] [[Category:WikiProject Ancient Egypt]][[Category:Priest Titles (Ancient Egypt)]][[Category:completion tier 2]] [[Category:WikiProject Ancient Egypt]][[Category:Priest Titles (Ancient Egypt)]][[Category:completion tier 2]] [[sn:Sem priest]] [[ja:セムとムトネフェル]][[de:Sem und Mut-nefer]] {{translated page|de|Sem (Priester)|version=223454035|comment=Imported full de history, revisions duplicated and version not here due to an error with my bot related to the German being under the same pagename as the english}} [[Category:Priestertitel (Altes Ägypten)]] [[fr:Prêtre-sem]] [[Category:Pages with no Wikidata]]

Duncker, Berlin 1923, Plates 13, 18; pp. 68–69. * Hermann Kees: ''Das Priestertum im ägyptischen Staat vom Neuen Reich bis zur Spätzeit''. Brill, Leiden 1953. * Christian Leitz et al.: ''Lexikon der ägyptischen Götter und Götterbezeichnungen''. Bd. 6: “H̱ - s”. Peeters, Leuven 2002, ISBN 90-429-1151-4, p. 306. * Flinders Petrie: ''Abydos I + II''. Brian Yare Egyptology, Worcester 2005 (Reprint 1902). * Heinrich Schäfer: ''Die Mysterien des Osiris in Abydos unter König Sesostris III – Nach dem Denkstein …''. Hinrichs, Leipzig 1904, pp. 15–19. * William A. Ward: ''Index of Egyptian administrative and religious titles of the Middle Kingdom: with a glossary …''. American University of Beirut, Beirut 1982, p. 168, No. 1465. == Weblinks == * [http://www.egyptology.com/reeder/enigma/tekenu8.html The Sem priest as panther-pelt wielder] == References == {{draft categories| [[Category:WikiProject Ancient Egypt]][[Category:Priest Titles (Ancient Egypt)]][[Category:completion tier 2]] [[Category:WikiProject Ancient Egypt]][[Category:Priest Titles (Ancient Egypt)]][[Category:completion tier 2]] }} [[sn:automatic wikipedia redirects]] [[sn:pages created from Interlanguage links]] [[fr:Pr%C3%AAtre-sem]] [[es:Sacerdote sem]] [[sn:Redirects to be cleared]] [[de:Sem (Priester)]][[ja:セム祭司]][[zh:瑟姆祭司]] [[Category:WikiProject Ancient Egypt]][[Category:Priest Titles (Ancient Egypt)]][[Category:completion tier 2]] [[Category:WikiProject Ancient Egypt]][[Category:Priest Titles (Ancient Egypt)]][[Category:completion tier 2]] [[Category:WikiProject Ancient Egypt]][[Category:Priest Titles (Ancient Egypt)]][[Category:completion tier 2]] [[sn:Sem priest]] [[ja:セムとムトネフェル]][[de:Sem und Mut-nefer]] {{translated page|de|Sem (Priester)|version=223454035|comment=Imported full de history, revisions duplicated and version not here due to an error with my bot related to the German being under the same pagename as the english}} [[Category:Priestertitel (Altes Ägypten)]] [[fr:Prêtre-sem]] [[Category:Pages with no Wikidata]] [[Category:Pages with no Wikidata]]
