Institut Français d'Archéologie Orientale, Cairo 1938, Plates 50 & 52.}} In Greco-Roman liturgy, [[:en:Seth (disambiguation)|Seth]] fled [[:en:Thoth|Thoth]] and [[:en:Anubis|Anubis]], shape-shifted into a panther, and was slain by Anubis. Anubis then entered Osiris’s embalming hall wearing the flayed pelt, declaring: “Seth is here.” == See also == * {{ill|Sem-em-iritef|de|Sem-em-iritef|ja|Sem-em-iritef|zh|Sem-em-iritef|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} * {{ill|Setem-atef-Usir|de|Setem-atef-Usir|ja|Setem-atef-Usir|zh|Setem-atef-Usir|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} * [[:en:Min festival|Min-Fest]] * {{ill|Ancient Egyptian administrative and functional titles|de|Altägyptische Beamten- und Funktionstitel|ja|古代エジプトの行政・官職称号|zh|古埃及行政与职能头衔|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} == Literature == * Friedrich-Wilhelm Bissing, Hermann Kees: ''Das Re-Heiligtum des Königs Ne-Woser-Re (Rathures), Bd. 2''. Duncker, Berlin 1923, Plates 13, 18; pp. 68–69. * Hermann Kees: ''Das Priestertum im ägyptischen Staat vom Neuen Reich bis zur Spätzeit''. Brill, Leiden 1953. * Christian Leitz et al.: ''Lexikon der ägyptischen Götter und Götterbezeichnungen''. Bd. 6: “H̱ - s”. Peeters, Leuven 2002, ISBN 90-429-1151-4, p. 306. * Flinders Petrie: ''Abydos I + II''. Brian Yare Egyptology, Worcester 2005 (Reprint 1902). * Heinrich Schäfer: ''Die Mysterien des Osiris in Abydos unter König Sesostris III – Nach dem Denkstein …''. Hinrichs, Leipzig 1904, pp. 15–19. * William A. Ward: ''Index of Egyptian administrative and religious titles of the Middle Kingdom: with a glossary …''. American University of Beirut, Beirut 1982, p. 168, No. 1465. == Weblinks == * [http://www.egyptology.com/reeder/enigma/tekenu8.html The Sem priest as panther-pelt wielder] == References == {{draft categories| [[Category:WikiProject Ancient Egypt]][[Category:Priest Titles (Ancient Egypt)]][[Category:completion tier 2]] [[Category:WikiProject Ancient Egypt]][[Category:Priest Titles (Ancient Egypt)]][[Category:completion tier 2]] }} [[fr:Pr%C3%AAtre-sem]] [[es:Sacerdote sem]] [[de:Sem (Priester)]][[ja:セム祭司]][[zh:瑟姆祭司]] [[Category:WikiProject Ancient Egypt]][[Category:Priest Titles (Ancient Egypt)]][[Category:completion tier 2]] [[Category:WikiProject Ancient Egypt]][[Category:Priest Titles (Ancient Egypt)]][[Category:completion tier 2]] [[Category:WikiProject Ancient Egypt]][[Category:Priest Titles (Ancient Egypt)]][[Category:completion tier 2]] [[ja:セムとムトネフェル]][[de:Sem und Mut-nefer]] {{translated page|de|Sem (Priester)|version=223454035|comment=Imported full de history, revisions duplicated and version not here due to an error with my bot related to the German being under the same pagename as the english}} [[Category:Priestertitel (Altes Ägypten)]] [[fr:Prêtre-sem]] {{wikidata link|Q1692364}} [[Category:Wikidata no short description]] [[Category:Wikidata has English label]] [[Category:qq]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]][[Category:qq]]

[[de:Sem (Priester)]][[es:Sacerdote sem]][[fr:Prêtre-sem]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[Category:Has wikidata]][[da:d:Q1692364]] Wikidata name: sem priest [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 20:24, 12 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|Wikidata entry Wikidata name: [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 05:26, 12 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|Wikidata entry Wikidata name: [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:10, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Infobox hieroglyphs two-columns |TITEL = Sem and Mut-nefer |NAME = G14-F35
Mut-nefer
''Mwt-nfr'' |NAME-ERWEITERT = [[:en:Early Dynastic Period (Egypt)|Early Dynastic Period]] |NAME2 = S29-G17
S29-X1-G17 |NAME2-ERWEITERT = [[:en:Middle Kingdom of Egypt|Middle Kingdom]] |NAME3 = S29-G17-A1
S29-X1-G17-A1
Sem / Setem
''Sm / Stm'' |NAME3-ERWEITERT = [[:en:New Kingdom of Egypt|New Kingdom]] |NAME4-ERWEITERT = Subtitle |NAME4 = R8-S20
{{ill|Chetemu-netjer|de|Chetemu-netjer|ja|チェテムネトジェル|zh|切特穆-内特哲|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}
''Ḫtmw-nṯr''
''Seal Keeper of the God''
also: ''Divine Sigillator'' }} '''Sem''' (also '''Setem''') was, in the [[:en:Old Kingdom of Egypt|Old Kingdom]], [[:en:Middle Kingdom of Egypt|Middle Kingdom]], and early [[:en:New Kingdom of Egypt|New Kingdom]], primarily the ancient Egyptian title for the “High Priest of [[:en:Osiris|Osiris]],” defined by the duties of the Sem priesthood in mortuary temples. The exact meaning of “Setem priest” in this context remains unclear. An ivory tag of [[:en:Den_(pharaoh)|King Den]] (1st Dynasty) provides the oldest iconographic evidence for the title Sem,Elisabeth Staehlin: ''Untersuchungen zur ägyptischen Tracht im Alten Reich''. Hessling, Berlin 1966, pp. 66–67, Plate XXXIV, Figure 38. then called ''Mut-nefer'' and still linked to the title {{ill|Tjet|de|Tjet|ja|トジェト|zh|杰特|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}. The Sem cult held a special position—it encompassed functions of the “High Priest of {{ill|Sameref|de|Sameref (ägyptische Mythologie)|ja|サメレフ|zh|萨梅里夫|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}” and underpinned worship of {{ill|Osiris-Chontamenti|de|Osiris-Chontamenti|ja|オシリス・コントァメンティ|zh|奥西里斯-孔塔门提|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} at [[:en:Abydos|Abydos]]. == Background == === Role assignment of the Sem priest === [[File:Egypt.Osiris.statuette.01.jpg|thumb|140px|Statue of Osiris, Egyptian Museum (Cairo)]] The Sem priest symbolizes the caring son of Osiris in relation to [[:en:Horus|Horus]], providing for his deceased father. On earth, the Sem priesthood assumed tasks of the {{ill|Semu|de|Semu|ja|セム神|zh|塞姆神|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} deities. As counterpart to the divine son {{ill|Sa-meref|de|Sameref|ja|サメレフ|zh|萨梅里夫|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, the Sem priest could revive the king, regenerate him, and secure his eternal rule. An inscription from the reign of [[:en:Thutmose III|Thutmose III]] by {{ill|Neb-waui|de|Neb-waui|ja|ネブワウイ|zh|内布-瓦维|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} reads: {{Quote|I performed my first office in the temple of Father Osiris. I served as Sa-meref in the Golden House rites, in the mysteries of the Lord of Abydos. I was skilled in adorning the god’s statue: a Sem priest with pure fingers. All craftsmen acted under my oversight.|Neb-waui|ref=Susan Eichler Selke: ''Die Verwaltung des „Hauses des Amun“ in der 18. Dynastie''. Buske, Hamburg 2000, pp. 191–192.}} During the [[:en:Eighteenth Dynasty of Egypt|18th Dynasty]], the {{ill|High Priest of Ptah|de|Wer-cherep-hemut|ja|プタハの大司祭|zh|普塔赫大祭司|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} also bore the title Sem priest, identifying with {{ill|Iunmutef|1=Iunmutef|2=de|3=Iunmutef|4=es|5=Iunmutef|6=fr|7=Iounmoutef|8=fi|9=Inmutef|de|Iunmutef|1=Iunmutef|2=de|3=Iunmutef|4=es|5=Iunmutef|6=fr|7=Iounmoutef|8=fi|9=Inmutef|ja|イウンムテフ|zh|尤努穆特夫|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}. Their garb gained the panther pelt and sidelock. Under [[:en:Amenhotep III|Amenhotep III]], we see the earliest Memphite high-priest title extended with Sem status: a statue of Ptahmose (“Image of Iunmutef”) son of Mencheper. The Sem priest also adopted the “King’s Son” title of {{ill|Horus Iunmutef|de|Horus Iunmutef|ja|ホルス・イウンムテフ|zh|荷鲁斯-尤努穆特夫|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}. Originally, in the [[:en:Old Kingdom of Egypt|Old Kingdom]], this role was reserved for the king’s eldest son; later, the Sem priest embodied the king’s son as “Observer at the ritual purification of the deceased,” linking directly to the deity [[:en:SEM|Sem]]. === Activities of the Sem priest === The Sem priest officiated the [[:en:Opening of the mouth ceremony|Opening of the mouth ceremony]], depicted in panther pelt, and mediated between king and gods. He dressed the king, allocated offerings, and during [[:en:Sed festival|Sed festival]] processions renewed royal power. In the temple vestibule of [[:en:Pepi II Neferkare|Pepi II]], the west wall shows the Sem offering linen, incense, and oil to the shrine gods, confirming the king as “First of the living”: {{Quote|The gods come, bringing ‘life, power, and health for the son of his body Nebmaatre’ and promise: ‘He celebrates the New Year festival with a broad heart, renews the months in the Nehebkau festival, and performs many Sed festivals on the throne like Re.’|West wall of Pepi II|ref=Gustave Jéquier: ''Le monument funéraire de Pepi II, Service des Antiquités de l'Égypte, Bd. 2: Le temple''. Institut Français d'Archéologie Orientale, Cairo 1938, Plates 50 & 52.}} In Greco-Roman liturgy, [[:en:Seth (disambiguation)|Seth]] fled [[:en:Thoth|Thoth]] and [[:en:Anubis|Anubis]], shape-shifted into a panther, and was slain by Anubis. Anubis then entered Osiris’s embalming hall wearing the flayed pelt, declaring: “Seth is here.” == See also == * {{ill|Sem-em-iritef|de|Sem-em-iritef|ja|Sem-em-iritef|zh|Sem-em-iritef|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} * {{ill|Setem-atef-Usir|de|Setem-atef-Usir|ja|Setem-atef-Usir|zh|Setem-atef-Usir|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} * [[:en:Min festival|Min-Fest]] * {{ill|Ancient Egyptian administrative and functional titles|de|Altägyptische Beamten- und Funktionstitel|ja|古代エジプトの行政・官職称号|zh|古埃及行政与职能头衔|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} == Literature == * Friedrich-Wilhelm Bissing, Hermann Kees: ''Das Re-Heiligtum des Königs Ne-Woser-Re (Rathures), Bd. 2''. Duncker, Berlin 1923, Plates 13, 18; pp. 68–69. * Hermann Kees: ''Das Priestertum im ägyptischen Staat vom Neuen Reich bis zur Spätzeit''. Brill, Leiden 1953. * Christian Leitz et al.: ''Lexikon der ägyptischen Götter und Götterbezeichnungen''. Bd. 6: “H̱ - s”. Peeters, Leuven 2002, ISBN 90-429-1151-4, p. 306. * Flinders Petrie: ''Abydos I + II''. Brian Yare Egyptology, Worcester 2005 (Reprint 1902). * Heinrich Schäfer: ''Die Mysterien des Osiris in Abydos unter König Sesostris III – Nach dem Denkstein …''. Hinrichs, Leipzig 1904, pp. 15–19. * William A. Ward: ''Index of Egyptian administrative and religious titles of the Middle Kingdom: with a glossary …''. American University of Beirut, Beirut 1982, p. 168, No. 1465. == Weblinks == * [http://www.egyptology.com/reeder/enigma/tekenu8.html The Sem priest as panther-pelt wielder] == References == {{draft categories| [[Category:WikiProject Ancient Egypt]][[Category:Priest Titles (Ancient Egypt)]][[Category:completion tier 2]] [[Category:WikiProject Ancient Egypt]][[Category:Priest Titles (Ancient Egypt)]][[Category:completion tier 2]] }} [[fr:Pr%C3%AAtre-sem]] [[es:Sacerdote sem]] [[de:Sem (Priester)]][[ja:セム祭司]][[zh:瑟姆祭司]] [[Category:WikiProject Ancient Egypt]][[Category:Priest Titles (Ancient Egypt)]][[Category:completion tier 2]] [[Category:WikiProject Ancient Egypt]][[Category:Priest Titles (Ancient Egypt)]][[Category:completion tier 2]] [[Category:WikiProject Ancient Egypt]][[Category:Priest Titles (Ancient Egypt)]][[Category:completion tier 2]] [[ja:セムとムトネフェル]][[de:Sem und Mut-nefer]] {{translated page|de|Sem (Priester)|version=223454035|comment=Imported full de history, revisions duplicated and version not here due to an error with my bot related to the German being under the same pagename as the english}} [[Category:Priestertitel (Altes Ägypten)]] [[fr:Prêtre-sem]] {{wikidata link|Q1692364}} [[Category:Wikidata no short description]] [[Category:Wikidata has English label]]

The Sem cult held a special position—it encompassed functions of the “High Priest of {{ill|Sameref|de|Sameref (ägyptische Mythologie)|ja|サメレフ|zh|萨梅里夫|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}” and underpinned worship of {{ill|Osiris-Chontamenti|de|Osiris-Chontamenti|ja|オシリス・コントァメンティ|zh|奥西里斯-孔塔门提|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} at [[:en:Abydos|Abydos]]. == Background == === Role assignment of the Sem priest === [[File:Egypt.Osiris.statuette.01.jpg|thumb|140px|Statue of Osiris, Egyptian Museum (Cairo)]] The Sem priest symbolizes the caring son of Osiris in relation to [[:en:Horus|Horus]], providing for his deceased father. On earth, the Sem priesthood assumed tasks of the {{ill|Semu|de|Semu|ja|セム神|zh|塞姆神|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} deities. As counterpart to the divine son {{ill|Sa-meref|de|Sameref|ja|サメレフ|zh|萨梅里夫|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, the Sem priest could revive the king, regenerate him, and secure his eternal rule. An inscription from the reign of [[:en:Thutmose III|Thutmose III]] by {{ill|Neb-waui|de|Neb-waui|ja|ネブワウイ|zh|内布-瓦维|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} reads: {{Quote|I performed my first office in the temple of Father Osiris. I served as Sa-meref in the Golden House rites, in the mysteries of the Lord of Abydos. I was skilled in adorning the god’s statue: a Sem priest with pure fingers. All craftsmen acted under my oversight.|Neb-waui|ref=Susan Eichler Selke: ''Die Verwaltung des „Hauses des Amun“ in der 18. Dynastie''. Buske, Hamburg 2000, pp. 191–192.}} During the [[:en:Eighteenth Dynasty of Egypt|18th Dynasty]], the {{ill|High Priest of Ptah|de|Wer-cherep-hemut|ja|プタハの大司祭|zh|普塔赫大祭司|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} also bore the title Sem priest, identifying with {{ill|Iunmutef|1=Iunmutef|2=de|3=Iunmutef|4=es|5=Iunmutef|6=fr|7=Iounmoutef|8=fi|9=Inmutef|de|Iunmutef|1=Iunmutef|2=de|3=Iunmutef|4=es|5=Iunmutef|6=fr|7=Iounmoutef|8=fi|9=Inmutef|ja|イウンムテフ|zh|尤努穆特夫|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}. Their garb gained the panther pelt and sidelock. Under [[:en:Amenhotep III|Amenhotep III]], we see the earliest Memphite high-priest title extended with Sem status: a statue of Ptahmose (“Image of Iunmutef”) son of Mencheper. The Sem priest also adopted the “King’s Son” title of {{ill|Horus Iunmutef|de|Horus Iunmutef|ja|ホルス・イウンムテフ|zh|荷鲁斯-尤努穆特夫|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}. Originally, in the [[:en:Old Kingdom of Egypt|Old Kingdom]], this role was reserved for the king’s eldest son; later, the Sem priest embodied the king’s son as “Observer at the ritual purification of the deceased,” linking directly to the deity [[:en:SEM|Sem]]. === Activities of the Sem priest === The Sem priest officiated the [[:en:Opening of the mouth ceremony|Opening of the mouth ceremony]], depicted in panther pelt, and mediated between king and gods. He dressed the king, allocated offerings, and during [[:en:Sed festival|Sed festival]] processions renewed royal power. In the temple vestibule of [[:en:Pepi II Neferkare|Pepi II]], the west wall shows the Sem offering linen, incense, and oil to the shrine gods, confirming the king as “First of the living”: {{Quote|The gods come, bringing ‘life, power, and health for the son of his body Nebmaatre’ and promise: ‘He celebrates the New Year festival with a broad heart, renews the months in the Nehebkau festival, and performs many Sed festivals on the throne like Re.’|West wall of Pepi II|ref=Gustave Jéquier: ''Le monument funéraire de Pepi II, Service des Antiquités de l'Égypte, Bd. 2: Le temple''. Institut Français d'Archéologie Orientale, Cairo 1938, Plates 50 & 52.}} In Greco-Roman liturgy, [[:en:Seth (disambiguation)|Seth]] fled [[:en:Thoth|Thoth]] and [[:en:Anubis|Anubis]], shape-shifted into a panther, and was slain by Anubis. Anubis then entered Osiris’s embalming hall wearing the flayed pelt, declaring: “Seth is here.” == See also == * {{ill|Sem-em-iritef|de|Sem-em-iritef|ja|Sem-em-iritef|zh|Sem-em-iritef|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} * {{ill|Setem-atef-Usir|de|Setem-atef-Usir|ja|Setem-atef-Usir|zh|Setem-atef-Usir|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} * [[:en:Min festival|Min-Fest]] * {{ill|Ancient Egyptian administrative and functional titles|de|Altägyptische Beamten- und Funktionstitel|ja|古代エジプトの行政・官職称号|zh|古埃及行政与职能头衔|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} == Literature == * Friedrich-Wilhelm Bissing, Hermann Kees: ''Das Re-Heiligtum des Königs Ne-Woser-Re (Rathures), Bd. 2''. Duncker, Berlin 1923, Plates 13, 18; pp. 68–69. * Hermann Kees: ''Das Priestertum im ägyptischen Staat vom Neuen Reich bis zur Spätzeit''. Brill, Leiden 1953. * Christian Leitz et al.: ''Lexikon der ägyptischen Götter und Götterbezeichnungen''. Bd. 6: “H̱ - s”. Peeters, Leuven 2002, ISBN 90-429-1151-4, p. 306. * Flinders Petrie: ''Abydos I + II''. Brian Yare Egyptology, Worcester 2005 (Reprint 1902). * Heinrich Schäfer: ''Die Mysterien des Osiris in Abydos unter König Sesostris III – Nach dem Denkstein …''. Hinrichs, Leipzig 1904, pp. 15–19. * William A. Ward: ''Index of Egyptian administrative and religious titles of the Middle Kingdom: with a glossary …''. American University of Beirut, Beirut 1982, p. 168, No. 1465. == Weblinks == * [http://www.egyptology.com/reeder/enigma/tekenu8.html The Sem priest as panther-pelt wielder] == References == {{draft categories| [[Category:WikiProject Ancient Egypt]][[Category:Priest Titles (Ancient Egypt)]][[Category:completion tier 2]] [[Category:WikiProject Ancient Egypt]][[Category:Priest Titles (Ancient Egypt)]][[Category:completion tier 2]] }} [[fr:Pr%C3%AAtre-sem]] [[es:Sacerdote sem]] [[de:Sem (Priester)]][[ja:セム祭司]][[zh:瑟姆祭司]] [[Category:WikiProject Ancient Egypt]][[Category:Priest Titles (Ancient Egypt)]][[Category:completion tier 2]] [[Category:WikiProject Ancient Egypt]][[Category:Priest Titles (Ancient Egypt)]][[Category:completion tier 2]] [[Category:WikiProject Ancient Egypt]][[Category:Priest Titles (Ancient Egypt)]][[Category:completion tier 2]] [[ja:セムとムトネフェル]][[de:Sem und Mut-nefer]] {{translated page|de|Sem (Priester)|version=223454035|comment=Imported full de history, revisions duplicated and version not here due to an error with my bot related to the German being under the same pagename as the english}} [[Category:Priestertitel (Altes Ägypten)]] [[fr:Prêtre-sem]] {{wikidata link|Q1692364}} [[Category:Wikidata no short description]] [[Category:Wikidata has English label]]

S29-X1-G17 |NAME2-ERWEITERT = [[:en:Middle Kingdom of Egypt|Middle Kingdom]] |NAME3 = S29-G17-A1

{{ill|Chetemu-netjer|de|Chetemu-netjer|ja|チェテムネトジェル|zh|切特穆-内特哲|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}
''Ḫtmw-nṯr''
''Seal Keeper of the God''
also: ''Divine Sigillator'' }} '''Sem''' (also '''Setem''') was, in the [[:en:Old Kingdom of Egypt|Old Kingdom]], [[:en:Middle Kingdom of Egypt|Middle Kingdom]], and early [[:en:New Kingdom of Egypt|New Kingdom]], primarily the ancient Egyptian title for the “High Priest of [[:en:Osiris|Osiris]],” defined by the duties of the Sem priesthood in mortuary temples. The exact meaning of “Setem priest” in this context remains unclear. An ivory tag of [[:en:Den_(pharaoh)|King Den]] (1st Dynasty) provides the oldest iconographic evidence for the title Sem,Elisabeth Staehlin: ''Untersuchungen zur ägyptischen Tracht im Alten Reich''. Hessling, Berlin 1966, pp. 66–67, Plate XXXIV, Figure 38. then called ''Mut-nefer'' and still linked to the title {{ill|Tjet|de|Tjet|ja|トジェト|zh|杰特|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}. The Sem cult held a special position—it encompassed functions of the “High Priest of {{ill|Sameref|de|Sameref (ägyptische Mythologie)|ja|サメレフ|zh|萨梅里夫|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}” and underpinned worship of {{ill|Osiris-Chontamenti|de|Osiris-Chontamenti|ja|オシリス・コントァメンティ|zh|奥西里斯-孔塔门提|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} at [[:en:Abydos|Abydos]]. == Background == === Role assignment of the Sem priest === [[File:Egypt.Osiris.statuette.01.jpg|thumb|140px|Statue of Osiris, Egyptian Museum (Cairo)]] The Sem priest symbolizes the caring son of Osiris in relation to [[:en:Horus|Horus]], providing for his deceased father. On earth, the Sem priesthood assumed tasks of the {{ill|Semu|de|Semu|ja|セム神|zh|塞姆神|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} deities. As counterpart to the divine son {{ill|Sa-meref|de|Sameref|ja|サメレフ|zh|萨梅里夫|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, the Sem priest could revive the king, regenerate him, and secure his eternal rule. An inscription from the reign of [[:en:Thutmose III|Thutmose III]] by {{ill|Neb-waui|de|Neb-waui|ja|ネブワウイ|zh|内布-瓦维|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} reads: {{Quote|I performed my first office in the temple of Father Osiris. I served as Sa-meref in the Golden House rites, in the mysteries of the Lord of Abydos. I was skilled in adorning the god’s statue: a Sem priest with pure fingers. All craftsmen acted under my oversight.|Neb-waui|ref=Susan Eichler Selke: ''Die Verwaltung des „Hauses des Amun“ in der 18. Dynastie''. Buske, Hamburg 2000, pp. 191–192.}} During the [[:en:Eighteenth Dynasty of Egypt|18th Dynasty]], the {{ill|High Priest of Ptah|de|Wer-cherep-hemut|ja|プタハの大司祭|zh|普塔赫大祭司|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} also bore the title Sem priest, identifying with {{ill|Iunmutef|1=Iunmutef|2=de|3=Iunmutef|4=es|5=Iunmutef|6=fr|7=Iounmoutef|8=fi|9=Inmutef|de|Iunmutef|1=Iunmutef|2=de|3=Iunmutef|4=es|5=Iunmutef|6=fr|7=Iounmoutef|8=fi|9=Inmutef|ja|イウンムテフ|zh|尤努穆特夫|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}. Their garb gained the panther pelt and sidelock. Under [[:en:Amenhotep III|Amenhotep III]], we see the earliest Memphite high-priest title extended with Sem status: a statue of Ptahmose (“Image of Iunmutef”) son of Mencheper. The Sem priest also adopted the “King’s Son” title of {{ill|Horus Iunmutef|de|Horus Iunmutef|ja|ホルス・イウンムテフ|zh|荷鲁斯-尤努穆特夫|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}. Originally, in the [[:en:Old Kingdom of Egypt|Old Kingdom]], this role was reserved for the king’s eldest son; later, the Sem priest embodied the king’s son as “Observer at the ritual purification of the deceased,” linking directly to the deity [[:en:SEM|Sem]]. === Activities of the Sem priest === The Sem priest officiated the [[:en:Opening of the mouth ceremony|Opening of the mouth ceremony]], depicted in panther pelt, and mediated between king and gods. He dressed the king, allocated offerings, and during [[:en:Sed festival|Sed festival]] processions renewed royal power. In the temple vestibule of [[:en:Pepi II Neferkare|Pepi II]], the west wall shows the Sem offering linen, incense, and oil to the shrine gods, confirming the king as “First of the living”: {{Quote|The gods come, bringing ‘life, power, and health for the son of his body Nebmaatre’ and promise: ‘He celebrates the New Year festival with a broad heart, renews the months in the Nehebkau festival, and performs many Sed festivals on the throne like Re.’|West wall of Pepi II|ref=Gustave Jéquier: ''Le monument funéraire de Pepi II, Service des Antiquités de l'Égypte, Bd. 2: Le temple''. Institut Français d'Archéologie Orientale, Cairo 1938, Plates 50 & 52.}} In Greco-Roman liturgy, [[:en:Seth (disambiguation)|Seth]] fled [[:en:Thoth|Thoth]] and [[:en:Anubis|Anubis]], shape-shifted into a panther, and was slain by Anubis. Anubis then entered Osiris’s embalming hall wearing the flayed pelt, declaring: “Seth is here.” == See also == * {{ill|Sem-em-iritef|de|Sem-em-iritef|ja|Sem-em-iritef|zh|Sem-em-iritef|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} * {{ill|Setem-atef-Usir|de|Setem-atef-Usir|ja|Setem-atef-Usir|zh|Setem-atef-Usir|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} * [[:en:Min festival|Min-Fest]] * {{ill|Ancient Egyptian administrative and functional titles|de|Altägyptische Beamten- und Funktionstitel|ja|古代エジプトの行政・官職称号|zh|古埃及行政与职能头衔|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} == Literature == * Friedrich-Wilhelm Bissing, Hermann Kees: ''Das Re-Heiligtum des Königs Ne-Woser-Re (Rathures), Bd. 2''. Duncker, Berlin 1923, Plates 13, 18; pp. 68–69. * Hermann Kees: ''Das Priestertum im ägyptischen Staat vom Neuen Reich bis zur Spätzeit''. Brill, Leiden 1953. * Christian Leitz et al.: ''Lexikon der ägyptischen Götter und Götterbezeichnungen''. Bd. 6: “H̱ - s”. Peeters, Leuven 2002, ISBN 90-429-1151-4, p. 306. * Flinders Petrie: ''Abydos I + II''. Brian Yare Egyptology, Worcester 2005 (Reprint 1902). * Heinrich Schäfer: ''Die Mysterien des Osiris in Abydos unter König Sesostris III – Nach dem Denkstein …''. Hinrichs, Leipzig 1904, pp. 15–19. * William A. Ward: ''Index of Egyptian administrative and religious titles of the Middle Kingdom: with a glossary …''. American University of Beirut, Beirut 1982, p. 168, No. 1465. == Weblinks == * [http://www.egyptology.com/reeder/enigma/tekenu8.html The Sem priest as panther-pelt wielder] == References == {{draft categories| }} [[fr:Pr%C3%AAtre-sem]] [[es:Sacerdote sem]] [[de:Sem (Priester)]][[ja:セム祭司]][[zh:瑟姆祭司]] [[ja:セムとムトネフェル]][[de:Sem und Mut-nefer]] {{translated page|de|Sem (Priester)|version=223454035|comment=Imported full de history, revisions duplicated and version not here due to an error with my bot related to the German being under the same pagename as the english}} [[fr:Prêtre-sem]] {{wikidata link|Q1692364}} [[Category:completion tier 2]] [[Category:Has wikidata]] [[Category:Priest Titles (Ancient Egypt)]] [[Category:Priestertitel (Altes Ägypten)]] [[Category:Wikidata has English label]] [[Category:Wikidata no short description]] [[Category:WikiProject Ancient Egypt]]

In the temple vestibule of [[:en:Pepi II Neferkare|Pepi II]], the west wall shows the Sem offering linen, incense, and oil to the shrine gods, confirming the king as “First of the living”: {{Quote|The gods come, bringing ‘life, power, and health for the son of his body Nebmaatre’ and promise: ‘He celebrates the New Year festival with a broad heart, renews the months in the Nehebkau festival, and performs many Sed festivals on the throne like Re.’|West wall of Pepi II|ref=Gustave Jéquier: ''Le monument funéraire de Pepi II, Service des Antiquités de l'Égypte, Bd. 2: Le temple''. Institut Français d'Archéologie Orientale, Cairo 1938, Plates 50 & 52.}} In Greco-Roman liturgy, [[:en:Seth (disambiguation)|Seth]] fled [[:en:Thoth|Thoth]] and [[:en:Anubis|Anubis]], shape-shifted into a panther, and was slain by Anubis. Anubis then entered Osiris’s embalming hall wearing the flayed pelt, declaring: “Seth is here.” == See also == * {{ill|Sem-em-iritef|de|Sem-em-iritef|ja|Sem-em-iritef|zh|Sem-em-iritef|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} * {{ill|Setem-atef-Usir|de|Setem-atef-Usir|ja|Setem-atef-Usir|zh|Setem-atef-Usir|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} * [[:en:Min festival|Min-Fest]] * {{ill|Ancient Egyptian administrative and functional titles|de|Altägyptische Beamten- und Funktionstitel|ja|古代エジプトの行政・官職称号|zh|古埃及行政与职能头衔|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} == Literature == * Friedrich-Wilhelm Bissing, Hermann Kees: ''Das Re-Heiligtum des Königs Ne-Woser-Re (Rathures), Bd. 2''. Duncker, Berlin 1923, Plates 13, 18; pp. 68–69. * Hermann Kees: ''Das Priestertum im ägyptischen Staat vom Neuen Reich bis zur Spätzeit''. Brill, Leiden 1953. * Christian Leitz et al.: ''Lexikon der ägyptischen Götter und Götterbezeichnungen''. Bd. 6: “H̱ - s”. Peeters, Leuven 2002, ISBN 90-429-1151-4, p. 306. * Flinders Petrie: ''Abydos I + II''. Brian Yare Egyptology, Worcester 2005 (Reprint 1902). * Heinrich Schäfer: ''Die Mysterien des Osiris in Abydos unter König Sesostris III – Nach dem Denkstein …''. Hinrichs, Leipzig 1904, pp. 15–19. * William A. Ward: ''Index of Egyptian administrative and religious titles of the Middle Kingdom: with a glossary …''. American University of Beirut, Beirut 1982, p. 168, No. 1465. == Weblinks == * [http://www.egyptology.com/reeder/enigma/tekenu8.html The Sem priest as panther-pelt wielder] == References == [[fr:Pr%C3%AAtre-sem]] [[es:Sacerdote sem]] [[de:Sem (Priester)]][[ja:セム祭司]][[zh:瑟姆祭司]] [[ja:セムとムトネフェル]][[de:Sem und Mut-nefer]] {{translated page|de|Sem (Priester)|version=223454035|comment=Imported full de history, revisions duplicated and version not here due to an error with my bot related to the German being under the same pagename as the english}} [[fr:Prêtre-sem]] {{wikidata link|Q1692364}} [[Category:completion tier 2]] [[Category:Has wikidata]] [[Category:Priest Titles (Ancient Egypt)]] [[Category:Priestertitel (Altes Ägypten)]] [[Category:Wikidata has English label]] [[Category:Wikidata no short description]] [[Category:WikiProject Ancient Egypt]]

Mut-nefer
''Mwt-nfr'' |NAME-ERWEITERT = [[:en:Early Dynastic Period (Egypt)|Early Dynastic Period]] |NAME2 = S29-G17
S29-X1-G17 |NAME2-ERWEITERT = [[:en:Middle Kingdom of Egypt|Middle Kingdom]] |NAME3 = S29-G17-A1
S29-X1-G17-A1

{{ill|Chetemu-netjer|de|Chetemu-netjer|ja|チェテムネトジェル|zh|切特穆-内特哲|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}
''Ḫtmw-nṯr''
''Seal Keeper of the God''
also: ''Divine Sigillator'' }} '''Sem''' (also '''Setem''') was, in the [[:en:Old Kingdom of Egypt|Old Kingdom]], [[:en:Middle Kingdom of Egypt|Middle Kingdom]], and early [[:en:New Kingdom of Egypt|New Kingdom]], primarily the ancient Egyptian title for the “High Priest of [[:en:Osiris|Osiris]],” defined by the duties of the Sem priesthood in mortuary temples. The exact meaning of “Setem priest” in this context remains unclear. An ivory tag of [[:en:Den_(pharaoh)|King Den]] (1st Dynasty) provides the oldest iconographic evidence for the title Sem,Elisabeth Staehlin: ''Untersuchungen zur ägyptischen Tracht im Alten Reich''. Hessling, Berlin 1966, pp. 66–67, Plate XXXIV, Figure 38. then called ''Mut-nefer'' and still linked to the title {{ill|Tjet|de|Tjet|ja|トジェト|zh|杰特|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}. The Sem cult held a special position—it encompassed functions of the “High Priest of {{ill|Sameref|de|Sameref (ägyptische Mythologie)|ja|サメレフ|zh|萨梅里夫|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}” and underpinned worship of {{ill|Osiris-Chontamenti|de|Osiris-Chontamenti|ja|オシリス・コントァメンティ|zh|奥西里斯-孔塔门提|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} at [[:en:Abydos|Abydos]]. == Background == === Role assignment of the Sem priest === [[File:Egypt.Osiris.statuette.01.jpg|thumb|140px|Statue of Osiris, Egyptian Museum (Cairo)]] The Sem priest symbolizes the caring son of Osiris in relation to [[:en:Horus|Horus]], providing for his deceased father. On earth, the Sem priesthood assumed tasks of the {{ill|Semu|de|Semu|ja|セム神|zh|塞姆神|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} deities. As counterpart to the divine son {{ill|Sa-meref|de|Sameref|ja|サメレフ|zh|萨梅里夫|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, the Sem priest could revive the king, regenerate him, and secure his eternal rule. An inscription from the reign of [[:en:Thutmose III|Thutmose III]] by {{ill|Neb-waui|de|Neb-waui|ja|ネブワウイ|zh|内布-瓦维|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} reads: {{Quote|I performed my first office in the temple of Father Osiris. I served as Sa-meref in the Golden House rites, in the mysteries of the Lord of Abydos. I was skilled in adorning the god’s statue: a Sem priest with pure fingers. All craftsmen acted under my oversight.|Neb-waui|ref=Susan Eichler Selke: ''Die Verwaltung des „Hauses des Amun“ in der 18. Dynastie''. Buske, Hamburg 2000, pp. 191–192.}} During the [[:en:Eighteenth Dynasty of Egypt|18th Dynasty]], the {{ill|High Priest of Ptah|de|Wer-cherep-hemut|ja|プタハの大司祭|zh|普塔赫大祭司|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} also bore the title Sem priest, identifying with {{ill|Iunmutef|1=Iunmutef|2=de|3=Iunmutef|4=es|5=Iunmutef|6=fr|7=Iounmoutef|8=fi|9=Inmutef|de|Iunmutef|1=Iunmutef|2=de|3=Iunmutef|4=es|5=Iunmutef|6=fr|7=Iounmoutef|8=fi|9=Inmutef|ja|イウンムテフ|zh|尤努穆特夫|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}. Their garb gained the panther pelt and sidelock. Under [[:en:Amenhotep III|Amenhotep III]], we see the earliest Memphite high-priest title extended with Sem status: a statue of Ptahmose (“Image of Iunmutef”) son of Mencheper. The Sem priest also adopted the “King’s Son” title of {{ill|Horus Iunmutef|de|Horus Iunmutef|ja|ホルス・イウンムテフ|zh|荷鲁斯-尤努穆特夫|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}. Originally, in the [[:en:Old Kingdom of Egypt|Old Kingdom]], this role was reserved for the king’s eldest son; later, the Sem priest embodied the king’s son as “Observer at the ritual purification of the deceased,” linking directly to the deity [[:en:SEM|Sem]]. === Activities of the Sem priest === The Sem priest officiated the [[:en:Opening of the mouth ceremony|Opening of the mouth ceremony]], depicted in panther pelt, and mediated between king and gods. He dressed the king, allocated offerings, and during [[:en:Sed festival|Sed festival]] processions renewed royal power. In the temple vestibule of [[:en:Pepi II Neferkare|Pepi II]], the west wall shows the Sem offering linen, incense, and oil to the shrine gods, confirming the king as “First of the living”: {{Quote|The gods come, bringing ‘life, power, and health for the son of his body Nebmaatre’ and promise: ‘He celebrates the New Year festival with a broad heart, renews the months in the Nehebkau festival, and performs many Sed festivals on the throne like Re.’|West wall of Pepi II|ref=Gustave Jéquier: ''Le monument funéraire de Pepi II, Service des Antiquités de l'Égypte, Bd. 2: Le temple''. Institut Français d'Archéologie Orientale, Cairo 1938, Plates 50 & 52.}} In Greco-Roman liturgy, [[:en:Seth (disambiguation)|Seth]] fled [[:en:Thoth|Thoth]] and [[:en:Anubis|Anubis]], shape-shifted into a panther, and was slain by Anubis. Anubis then entered Osiris’s embalming hall wearing the flayed pelt, declaring: “Seth is here.” == See also == * {{ill|Sem-em-iritef|de|Sem-em-iritef|ja|Sem-em-iritef|zh|Sem-em-iritef|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} * {{ill|Setem-atef-Usir|de|Setem-atef-Usir|ja|Setem-atef-Usir|zh|Setem-atef-Usir|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} * [[:en:Min festival|Min-Fest]] * {{ill|Ancient Egyptian administrative and functional titles|de|Altägyptische Beamten- und Funktionstitel|ja|古代エジプトの行政・官職称号|zh|古埃及行政与职能头衔|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} == Literature == * Friedrich-Wilhelm Bissing, Hermann Kees: ''Das Re-Heiligtum des Königs Ne-Woser-Re (Rathures), Bd. 2''. Duncker, Berlin 1923, Plates 13, 18; pp. 68–69. * Hermann Kees: ''Das Priestertum im ägyptischen Staat vom Neuen Reich bis zur Spätzeit''. Brill, Leiden 1953. * Christian Leitz et al.: ''Lexikon der ägyptischen Götter und Götterbezeichnungen''. Bd. 6: “H̱ - s”. Peeters, Leuven 2002, ISBN 90-429-1151-4, p. 306. * Flinders Petrie: ''Abydos I + II''. Brian Yare Egyptology, Worcester 2005 (Reprint 1902). * Heinrich Schäfer: ''Die Mysterien des Osiris in Abydos unter König Sesostris III – Nach dem Denkstein …''. Hinrichs, Leipzig 1904, pp. 15–19. * William A. Ward: ''Index of Egyptian administrative and religious titles of the Middle Kingdom: with a glossary …''. American University of Beirut, Beirut 1982, p. 168, No. 1465. == Weblinks == * [http://www.egyptology.com/reeder/enigma/tekenu8.html The Sem priest as panther-pelt wielder] == References == [[fr:Pr%C3%AAtre-sem]] [[es:Sacerdote sem]] [[de:Sem (Priester)]][[ja:セム祭司]][[zh:瑟姆祭司]] [[ja:セムとムトネフェル]][[de:Sem und Mut-nefer]] {{translated page|de|Sem (Priester)|version=223454035|comment=Imported full de history, revisions duplicated and version not here due to an error with my bot related to the German being under the same pagename as the english}} [[fr:Prêtre-sem]] {{wikidata link|Q1692364}} [[Category:completion tier 2]] [[Category:Priest Titles (Ancient Egypt)]] [[Category:Priestertitel (Altes Ägypten)]] [[Category:Wikidata has English label]] [[Category:Wikidata no short description]] [[Category:WikiProject Ancient Egypt]]

Originally, in the {{ill|Old Kingdom of Egypt|en|Old Kingdom of Egypt|WD=Q177819|lt=Old Kingdom}}, this role was reserved for the king’s eldest son; later, the Sem priest embodied the king’s son as “Observer at the ritual purification of the deceased,” linking directly to the deity {{ill|SEM|en|SEM|WD=Q233287|lt=Sem}}. === Activities of the Sem priest === The Sem priest officiated the {{ill|Opening of the mouth ceremony|en|Opening of the mouth ceremony|WD=Q1191513|lt=Opening of the mouth ceremony}}, depicted in panther pelt, and mediated between king and gods. also: ''Divine Sigillator'' }} '''Sem''' (also '''Setem''') was, in the {{ill|Old Kingdom of Egypt|en|Old Kingdom of Egypt|WD=Q177819|lt=Old Kingdom}}, {{ill|Middle Kingdom of Egypt|en|Middle Kingdom of Egypt|WD=Q191324|lt=Middle Kingdom}}, and early {{ill|New Kingdom of Egypt|en|New Kingdom of Egypt|WD=Q180568|lt=New Kingdom}}, primarily the ancient Egyptian title for the “High Priest of {{ill|Osiris|en|Osiris|WD=Q46491|lt=Osiris}},” defined by the duties of the Sem priesthood in mortuary temples. The exact meaning of “Setem priest” in this context remains unclear. An ivory tag of {{ill|Den_(pharaoh)|en|Den_(pharaoh)|WD=Q151822|lt=King Den}} (1st Dynasty) provides the oldest iconographic evidence for the title Sem,Elisabeth Staehlin: ''Untersuchungen zur ägyptischen Tracht im Alten Reich''. Hessling, Berlin 1966, pp. 66–67, Plate XXXIV, Figure 38. then called ''Mut-nefer'' and still linked to the title {{ill|Tjet|de|Tjet|ja|トジェト|zh|杰特|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}. The Sem cult held a special position—it encompassed functions of the “High Priest of {{ill|Sameref|de|Sameref (ägyptische Mythologie)|ja|サメレフ|zh|萨梅里夫|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}” and underpinned worship of {{ill|Osiris-Chontamenti|de|Osiris-Chontamenti|ja|オシリス・コントァメンティ|zh|奥西里斯-孔塔门提|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} at {{ill|Abydos|en|Abydos|WD=Q224842|lt=Abydos}}. == Background == === Role assignment of the Sem priest === [[File:Egypt.Osiris.statuette.01.jpg|thumb|140px|Statue of Osiris, Egyptian Museum (Cairo)]] The Sem priest symbolizes the caring son of Osiris in relation to {{ill|Horus|en|Horus|WD=Q84122|lt=Horus}}, providing for his deceased father. On earth, the Sem priesthood assumed tasks of the {{ill|Semu|de|Semu|ja|セム神|zh|塞姆神|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} deities. As counterpart to the divine son {{ill|Sa-meref|de|Sameref|ja|サメレフ|zh|萨梅里夫|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, the Sem priest could revive the king, regenerate him, and secure his eternal rule. An inscription from the reign of {{ill|Thutmose III|en|Thutmose III|WD=Q157899|lt=Thutmose III}} by {{ill|Neb-waui|de|Neb-waui|ja|ネブワウイ|zh|内布-瓦维|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} reads: {{Quote|I performed my first office in the temple of Father Osiris. I served as Sa-meref in the Golden House rites, in the mysteries of the Lord of Abydos. I was skilled in adorning the god’s statue: a Sem priest with pure fingers. All craftsmen acted under my oversight.|Neb-waui|ref=Susan Eichler Selke: ''Die Verwaltung des „Hauses des Amun“ in der 18. Dynastie''. Buske, Hamburg 2000, pp. 191–192.}} During the {{ill|Eighteenth Dynasty of Egypt|en|Eighteenth Dynasty of Egypt|WD=Q146055|lt=18th Dynasty}}, the {{ill|High Priest of Ptah|de|Wer-cherep-hemut|ja|プタハの大司祭|zh|普塔赫大祭司|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} also bore the title Sem priest, identifying with {{ill|Iunmutef|1=Iunmutef|2=de|3=Iunmutef|4=es|5=Iunmutef|6=fr|7=Iounmoutef|8=fi|9=Inmutef|de|Iunmutef|1=Iunmutef|2=de|3=Iunmutef|4=es|5=Iunmutef|6=fr|7=Iounmoutef|8=fi|9=Inmutef|ja|イウンムテフ|zh|尤努穆特夫|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}. Their garb gained the panther pelt and sidelock. Under {{ill|Amenhotep III|en|Amenhotep III|WD=Q42606|lt=Amenhotep III}}, we see the earliest Memphite high-priest title extended with Sem status: a statue of Ptahmose (“Image of Iunmutef”) son of Mencheper. The Sem priest also adopted the “King’s Son” title of {{ill|Horus Iunmutef|de|Horus Iunmutef|ja|ホルス・イウンムテフ|zh|荷鲁斯-尤努穆特夫|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}. Originally, in the {{ill|Old Kingdom of Egypt|en|Old Kingdom of Egypt|WD=Q177819|lt=Old Kingdom}}, this role was reserved for the king’s eldest son; later, the Sem priest embodied the king’s son as “Observer at the ritual purification of the deceased,” linking directly to the deity {{ill|SEM|en|SEM|WD=Q233287|lt=Sem}}. === Activities of the Sem priest === The Sem priest officiated the {{ill|Opening of the mouth ceremony|en|Opening of the mouth ceremony|WD=Q1191513|lt=Opening of the mouth ceremony}}, depicted in panther pelt, and mediated between king and gods. He dressed the king, allocated offerings, and during {{ill|Sed festival|en|Sed festival|WD=Q604644|lt=Sed festival}} processions renewed royal power. In the temple vestibule of {{ill|Pepi II Neferkare|en|Pepi II Neferkare|WD=Q6198|lt=Pepi II}}, the west wall shows the Sem offering linen, incense, and oil to the shrine gods, confirming the king as “First of the living”: {{Quote|The gods come, bringing ‘life, power, and health for the son of his body Nebmaatre’ and promise: ‘He celebrates the New Year festival with a broad heart, renews the months in the Nehebkau festival, and performs many Sed festivals on the throne like Re.’|West wall of Pepi II|ref=Gustave Jéquier: ''Le monument funéraire de Pepi II, Service des Antiquités de l'Égypte, Bd. 2: Le temple''. Institut Français d'Archéologie Orientale, Cairo 1938, Plates 50 & 52.}} In Greco-Roman liturgy, {{ill|Seth (disambiguation)|en|Seth (disambiguation)|WD=Q971389|lt=Seth}} fled {{ill|Thoth|en|Thoth|WD=Q146921|lt=Thoth}} and {{ill|Anubis|en|Anubis|WD=Q47534|lt=Anubis}}, shape-shifted into a panther, and was slain by Anubis. Anubis then entered Osiris’s embalming hall wearing the flayed pelt, declaring: “Seth is here.” == See also == * {{ill|Sem-em-iritef|de|Sem-em-iritef|ja|Sem-em-iritef|zh|Sem-em-iritef|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} * {{ill|Setem-atef-Usir|de|Setem-atef-Usir|ja|Setem-atef-Usir|zh|Setem-atef-Usir|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} * {{ill|Min festival|en|Min festival|WD=Q1936130|lt=Min-Fest}} * {{ill|Ancient Egyptian administrative and functional titles|de|Altägyptische Beamten- und Funktionstitel|ja|古代エジプトの行政・官職称号|zh|古埃及行政与职能头衔|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} == Literature == * Friedrich-Wilhelm Bissing, Hermann Kees: ''Das Re-Heiligtum des Königs Ne-Woser-Re (Rathures), Bd. 2''. Duncker, Berlin 1923, Plates 13, 18; pp. 68–69. * Hermann Kees: ''Das Priestertum im ägyptischen Staat vom Neuen Reich bis zur Spätzeit''. Brill, Leiden 1953. * Christian Leitz et al.: ''Lexikon der ägyptischen Götter und Götterbezeichnungen''. Bd. 6: “H̱ - s”. Peeters, Leuven 2002, ISBN 90-429-1151-4, p. 306. * Flinders Petrie: ''Abydos I + II''. Brian Yare Egyptology, Worcester 2005 (Reprint 1902). * Heinrich Schäfer: ''Die Mysterien des Osiris in Abydos unter König Sesostris III – Nach dem Denkstein …''. Hinrichs, Leipzig 1904, pp. 15–19. * William A. Ward: ''Index of Egyptian administrative and religious titles of the Middle Kingdom: with a glossary …''. American University of Beirut, Beirut 1982, p. 168, No. 1465. == Weblinks == * [http://www.egyptology.com/reeder/enigma/tekenu8.html The Sem priest as panther-pelt wielder] == References == [[fr:Pr%C3%AAtre-sem]] [[es:Sacerdote sem]] [[de:Sem (Priester)]][[ja:セム祭司]][[zh:瑟姆祭司]] [[ja:セムとムトネフェル]][[de:Sem und Mut-nefer]] {{translated page|de|Sem (Priester)|version=223454035|comment=Imported full de history, revisions duplicated and version not here due to an error with my bot related to the German being under the same pagename as the english}} [[fr:Prêtre-sem]] {{wikidata link|Q1692364}} [[Category:completion tier 2]] [[Category:Priest Titles (Ancient Egypt)]] [[Category:Priestertitel (Altes Ägypten)]] [[Category:qq]] [[Category:Wikidata no short description]] [[Category:WikiProject Ancient Egypt]]

He dressed the king, allocated offerings, and during {{ill|Sed festival|en|Sed festival|WD=Q604644|lt=Sed festival}} processions renewed royal power. In the temple vestibule of {{ill|Pepi II Neferkare|en|Pepi II Neferkare|WD=Q6198|lt=Pepi II}}, the west wall shows the Sem offering linen, incense, and oil to the shrine gods, confirming the king as “First of the living”: {{Quote|The gods come, bringing ‘life, power, and health for the son of his body Nebmaatre’ and promise: ‘He celebrates the New Year festival with a broad heart, renews the months in the Nehebkau festival, and performs many Sed festivals on the throne like Re.’|West wall of Pepi II|ref=Gustave Jéquier: ''Le monument funéraire de Pepi II, Service des Antiquités de l'Égypte, Bd. 2: Le temple''. Institut Français d'Archéologie Orientale, Cairo 1938, Plates 50 & 52.}} In Greco-Roman liturgy, {{ill|Seth (disambiguation)|en|Seth (disambiguation)|WD=Q971389|lt=Seth}} fled {{ill|Thoth|en|Thoth|WD=Q146921|lt=Thoth}} and {{ill|Anubis|en|Anubis|WD=Q47534|lt=Anubis}}, shape-shifted into a panther, and was slain by Anubis. Anubis then entered Osiris’s embalming hall wearing the flayed pelt, declaring: “Seth is here.” == See also == * {{ill|Sem-em-iritef|de|Sem-em-iritef|ja|Sem-em-iritef|zh|Sem-em-iritef|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} * {{ill|Setem-atef-Usir|de|Setem-atef-Usir|ja|Setem-atef-Usir|zh|Setem-atef-Usir|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} * {{ill|Min festival|en|Min festival|WD=Q1936130|lt=Min-Fest}} * {{ill|Ancient Egyptian administrative and functional titles|de|Altägyptische Beamten- und Funktionstitel|ja|古代エジプトの行政・官職称号|zh|古埃及行政与职能头衔|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} == Literature == * Friedrich-Wilhelm Bissing, Hermann Kees: ''Das Re-Heiligtum des Königs Ne-Woser-Re (Rathures), Bd. 2''. Duncker, Berlin 1923, Plates 13, 18; pp. 68–69. * Hermann Kees: ''Das Priestertum im ägyptischen Staat vom Neuen Reich bis zur Spätzeit''. Brill, Leiden 1953. * Christian Leitz et al.: ''Lexikon der ägyptischen Götter und Götterbezeichnungen''. Bd. 6: “H̱ - s”. Peeters, Leuven 2002, ISBN 90-429-1151-4, p. 306. * Flinders Petrie: ''Abydos I + II''. Brian Yare Egyptology, Worcester 2005 (Reprint 1902). * Heinrich Schäfer: ''Die Mysterien des Osiris in Abydos unter König Sesostris III – Nach dem Denkstein …''. Hinrichs, Leipzig 1904, pp. 15–19. * William A. Ward: ''Index of Egyptian administrative and religious titles of the Middle Kingdom: with a glossary …''. American University of Beirut, Beirut 1982, p. 168, No. 1465. == Weblinks == * [http://www.egyptology.com/reeder/enigma/tekenu8.html The Sem priest as panther-pelt wielder] == References == [[fr:Pr%C3%AAtre-sem]] [[es:Sacerdote sem]] [[de:Sem (Priester)]][[ja:セム祭司]][[zh:瑟姆祭司]] [[ja:セムとムトネフェル]][[de:Sem und Mut-nefer]] {{translated page|de|Sem (Priester)|version=223454035|comment=Imported full de history, revisions duplicated and version not here due to an error with my bot related to the German being under the same pagename as the english}} [[fr:Prêtre-sem]] {{wikidata link|Q1692364}} [[Category:completion tier 2]] [[Category:Priest Titles (Ancient Egypt)]] [[Category:Priestertitel (Altes Ägypten)]] [[Category:Wikidata no short description]] [[Category:WikiProject Ancient Egypt]]

[[de:Sem (Priester)]][[es:Sacerdote sem]][[fr:Prêtre-sem]] [[de:Sem (Priester)]][[es:Sacerdote sem]][[fr:Prêtre-sem]] [[de:Sem (Priester)]][[es:Sacerdote sem]][[fr:Prêtre-sem]] {{Infobox hieroglyphs two-columns |TITEL = Sem and Mut-nefer |NAME = G14-F35
Mut-nefer
''Mwt-nfr'' |NAME-ERWEITERT = {{ill|Early Dynastic Period (Egypt)|en|Early Dynastic Period (Egypt)|WD=Q187979|lt=Early Dynastic Period}} |NAME2 = S29-G17
S29-X1-G17 |NAME2-ERWEITERT = {{ill|Middle Kingdom of Egypt|en|Middle Kingdom of Egypt|WD=Q191324|lt=Middle Kingdom}} |NAME3 = S29-G17-A1
S29-X1-G17-A1
Sem / Setem
''Sm / Stm'' |NAME3-ERWEITERT = {{ill|New Kingdom of Egypt|en|New Kingdom of Egypt|WD=Q180568|lt=New Kingdom}} |NAME4-ERWEITERT = Subtitle |NAME4 = R8-S20
{{ill|Chetemu-netjer|de|Chetemu-netjer|ja|チェテムネトジェル|zh|切特穆-内特哲|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}
''Ḫtmw-nṯr''
''Seal Keeper of the God''
also: ''Divine Sigillator'' }} '''Sem''' (also '''Setem''') was, in the {{ill|Old Kingdom of Egypt|en|Old Kingdom of Egypt|WD=Q177819|lt=Old Kingdom}}, {{ill|Middle Kingdom of Egypt|en|Middle Kingdom of Egypt|WD=Q191324|lt=Middle Kingdom}}, and early {{ill|New Kingdom of Egypt|en|New Kingdom of Egypt|WD=Q180568|lt=New Kingdom}}, primarily the ancient Egyptian title for the “High Priest of {{ill|Osiris|en|Osiris|WD=Q46491|lt=Osiris}},” defined by the duties of the Sem priesthood in mortuary temples. The exact meaning of “Setem priest” in this context remains unclear. An ivory tag of {{ill|Den_(pharaoh)|en|Den_(pharaoh)|WD=Q151822|lt=King Den}} (1st Dynasty) provides the oldest iconographic evidence for the title Sem,Elisabeth Staehlin: ''Untersuchungen zur ägyptischen Tracht im Alten Reich''. Hessling, Berlin 1966, pp. 66–67, Plate XXXIV, Figure 38. then called ''Mut-nefer'' and still linked to the title {{ill|Tjet|de|Tjet|ja|トジェト|zh|杰特|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}. The Sem cult held a special position—it encompassed functions of the “High Priest of {{ill|Sameref|de|Sameref (ägyptische Mythologie)|ja|サメレフ|zh|萨梅里夫|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}” and underpinned worship of {{ill|Osiris-Chontamenti|de|Osiris-Chontamenti|ja|オシリス・コントァメンティ|zh|奥西里斯-孔塔门提|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} at {{ill|Abydos|en|Abydos|WD=Q224842|lt=Abydos}}. == Background == === Role assignment of the Sem priest === [[File:Egypt.Osiris.statuette.01.jpg|thumb|140px|Statue of Osiris, Egyptian Museum (Cairo)]] The Sem priest symbolizes the caring son of Osiris in relation to {{ill|Horus|en|Horus|WD=Q84122|lt=Horus}}, providing for his deceased father. On earth, the Sem priesthood assumed tasks of the {{ill|Semu|de|Semu|ja|セム神|zh|塞姆神|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} deities. As counterpart to the divine son {{ill|Sa-meref|de|Sameref|ja|サメレフ|zh|萨梅里夫|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, the Sem priest could revive the king, regenerate him, and secure his eternal rule. An inscription from the reign of {{ill|Thutmose III|en|Thutmose III|WD=Q157899|lt=Thutmose III}} by {{ill|Neb-waui|de|Neb-waui|ja|ネブワウイ|zh|内布-瓦维|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} reads: {{Quote|I performed my first office in the temple of Father Osiris. I served as Sa-meref in the Golden House rites, in the mysteries of the Lord of Abydos. I was skilled in adorning the god’s statue: a Sem priest with pure fingers. All craftsmen acted under my oversight.|Neb-waui|ref=Susan Eichler Selke: ''Die Verwaltung des „Hauses des Amun“ in der 18. Dynastie''. Buske, Hamburg 2000, pp. 191–192.}} During the {{ill|Eighteenth Dynasty of Egypt|en|Eighteenth Dynasty of Egypt|WD=Q146055|lt=18th Dynasty}}, the {{ill|High Priest of Ptah|de|Wer-cherep-hemut|ja|プタハの大司祭|zh|普塔赫大祭司|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} also bore the title Sem priest, identifying with {{ill|Iunmutef|1=Iunmutef|2=de|3=Iunmutef|4=es|5=Iunmutef|6=fr|7=Iounmoutef|8=fi|9=Inmutef|de|Iunmutef|1=Iunmutef|2=de|3=Iunmutef|4=es|5=Iunmutef|6=fr|7=Iounmoutef|8=fi|9=Inmutef|ja|イウンムテフ|zh|尤努穆特夫|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}. Their garb gained the panther pelt and sidelock. Under {{ill|Amenhotep III|en|Amenhotep III|WD=Q42606|lt=Amenhotep III}}, we see the earliest Memphite high-priest title extended with Sem status: a statue of Ptahmose (“Image of Iunmutef”) son of Mencheper. The Sem priest also adopted the “King’s Son” title of {{ill|Horus Iunmutef|de|Horus Iunmutef|ja|ホルス・イウンムテフ|zh|荷鲁斯-尤努穆特夫|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}. Originally, in the {{ill|Old Kingdom of Egypt|en|Old Kingdom of Egypt|WD=Q177819|lt=Old Kingdom}}, this role was reserved for the king’s eldest son; later, the Sem priest embodied the king’s son as “Observer at the ritual purification of the deceased,” linking directly to the deity {{ill|SEM|en|SEM|WD=Q233287|lt=Sem}}. === Activities of the Sem priest === The Sem priest officiated the {{ill|Opening of the mouth ceremony|en|Opening of the mouth ceremony|WD=Q1191513|lt=Opening of the mouth ceremony}}, depicted in panther pelt, and mediated between king and gods. He dressed the king, allocated offerings, and during {{ill|Sed festival|en|Sed festival|WD=Q604644|lt=Sed festival}} processions renewed royal power. In the temple vestibule of {{ill|Pepi II Neferkare|en|Pepi II Neferkare|WD=Q6198|lt=Pepi II}}, the west wall shows the Sem offering linen, incense, and oil to the shrine gods, confirming the king as “First of the living”: {{Quote|The gods come, bringing ‘life, power, and health for the son of his body Nebmaatre’ and promise: ‘He celebrates the New Year festival with a broad heart, renews the months in the Nehebkau festival, and performs many Sed festivals on the throne like Re.’|West wall of Pepi II|ref=Gustave Jéquier: ''Le monument funéraire de Pepi II, Service des Antiquités de l'Égypte, Bd. 2: Le temple''. Institut Français d'Archéologie Orientale, Cairo 1938, Plates 50 & 52.}} In Greco-Roman liturgy, {{ill|Seth (disambiguation)|en|Seth (disambiguation)|WD=Q971389|lt=Seth}} fled {{ill|Thoth|en|Thoth|WD=Q146921|lt=Thoth}} and {{ill|Anubis|en|Anubis|WD=Q47534|lt=Anubis}}, shape-shifted into a panther, and was slain by Anubis. Anubis then entered Osiris’s embalming hall wearing the flayed pelt, declaring: “Seth is here.” == See also == * {{ill|Sem-em-iritef|de|Sem-em-iritef|ja|Sem-em-iritef|zh|Sem-em-iritef|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} * {{ill|Setem-atef-Usir|de|Setem-atef-Usir|ja|Setem-atef-Usir|zh|Setem-atef-Usir|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} * {{ill|Min festival|en|Min festival|WD=Q1936130|lt=Min-Fest}} * {{ill|Ancient Egyptian administrative and functional titles|de|Altägyptische Beamten- und Funktionstitel|ja|古代エジプトの行政・官職称号|zh|古埃及行政与职能头衔|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} == Literature == * Friedrich-Wilhelm Bissing, Hermann Kees: ''Das Re-Heiligtum des Königs Ne-Woser-Re (Rathures), Bd. 2''. Duncker, Berlin 1923, Plates 13, 18; pp. 68–69. * Hermann Kees: ''Das Priestertum im ägyptischen Staat vom Neuen Reich bis zur Spätzeit''. Brill, Leiden 1953. * Christian Leitz et al.: ''Lexikon der ägyptischen Götter und Götterbezeichnungen''. Bd. 6: “H̱ - s”. Peeters, Leuven 2002, ISBN 90-429-1151-4, p. 306. * Flinders Petrie: ''Abydos I + II''. Brian Yare Egyptology, Worcester 2005 (Reprint 1902). * Heinrich Schäfer: ''Die Mysterien des Osiris in Abydos unter König Sesostris III – Nach dem Denkstein …''. Hinrichs, Leipzig 1904, pp. 15–19. * William A. Ward: ''Index of Egyptian administrative and religious titles of the Middle Kingdom: with a glossary …''. American University of Beirut, Beirut 1982, p. 168, No. 1465. == Weblinks == * [http://www.egyptology.com/reeder/enigma/tekenu8.html The Sem priest as panther-pelt wielder] == References == [[fr:Pr%C3%AAtre-sem]] [[es:Sacerdote sem]] [[de:Sem (Priester)]][[ja:セム祭司]][[zh:瑟姆祭司]] [[ja:セムとムトネフェル]][[de:Sem und Mut-nefer]] {{translated page|de|Sem (Priester)|version=223454035|comment=Imported full de history, revisions duplicated and version not here due to an error with my bot related to the German being under the same pagename as the english}} [[fr:Prêtre-sem]] {{wikidata link|Q1692364}} [[Category:completion tier 2]] [[Category:Priest Titles (Ancient Egypt)]] [[Category:Priestertitel (Altes Ägypten)]] [[Category:Wikidata no short description]] [[Category:WikiProject Ancient Egypt]] [[Category:pages]]

also: ''Divine Sigillator'' }} '''Sem''' (also '''Setem''') was, in the {{ill|Old Kingdom of Egypt|en|Old Kingdom of Egypt|WD=Q177819|lt=Old Kingdom|1=Old Kingdom of Egypt}}, {{ill|Middle Kingdom of Egypt|en|Middle Kingdom of Egypt|WD=Q191324|lt=Middle Kingdom|1=Middle Kingdom of Egypt}}, and early {{ill|New Kingdom of Egypt|en|New Kingdom of Egypt|WD=Q180568|lt=New Kingdom|1=New Kingdom of Egypt}}, primarily the ancient Egyptian title for the “High Priest of {{ill|Osiris|en|Osiris|WD=Q46491|lt=Osiris|1=Osiris}},” defined by the duties of the Sem priesthood in mortuary temples. The exact meaning of “Setem priest” in this context remains unclear. An ivory tag of {{ill|Den_(pharaoh)|en|Den_(pharaoh)|WD=Q151822|lt=King Den|1=Den (pharaoh)}} (1st Dynasty) provides the oldest iconographic evidence for the title Sem,Elisabeth Staehlin: ''Untersuchungen zur ägyptischen Tracht im Alten Reich''. Hessling, Berlin 1966, pp. 66–67, Plate XXXIV, Figure 38. then called ''Mut-nefer'' and still linked to the title {{ill|Tjet|de|Tjet|ja|トジェト|zh|杰特|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}. The Sem cult held a special position—it encompassed functions of the “High Priest of {{ill|Sameref|de|Sameref (ägyptische Mythologie)|ja|サメレフ|zh|萨梅里夫|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}” and underpinned worship of {{ill|Osiris-Chontamenti|de|Osiris-Chontamenti|ja|オシリス・コントァメンティ|zh|奥西里斯-孔塔门提|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} at {{ill|Abydos|en|Abydos|WD=Q224842|lt=Abydos|1=Abydos}}. == Background == === Role assignment of the Sem priest === [[File:Egypt.Osiris.statuette.01.jpg|thumb|140px|Statue of Osiris, Egyptian Museum (Cairo)]] The Sem priest symbolizes the caring son of Osiris in relation to {{ill|Horus|en|Horus|WD=Q84122|lt=Horus|1=Horus}}, providing for his deceased father. On earth, the Sem priesthood assumed tasks of the {{ill|Semu|de|Semu|ja|セム神|zh|塞姆神|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} deities. As counterpart to the divine son {{ill|Sa-meref|de|Sameref|ja|サメレフ|zh|萨梅里夫|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, the Sem priest could revive the king, regenerate him, and secure his eternal rule. An inscription from the reign of {{ill|Thutmose III|en|Thutmose III|WD=Q157899|lt=Thutmose III|1=Thutmose III}} by {{ill|Neb-waui|de|Neb-waui|ja|ネブワウイ|zh|内布-瓦维|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} reads: {{Quote|I performed my first office in the temple of Father Osiris. I served as Sa-meref in the Golden House rites, in the mysteries of the Lord of Abydos. I was skilled in adorning the god’s statue: a Sem priest with pure fingers. All craftsmen acted under my oversight.|Neb-waui|ref=Susan Eichler Selke: ''Die Verwaltung des „Hauses des Amun“ in der 18. Dynastie''. Buske, Hamburg 2000, pp. 191–192.}} During the {{ill|Eighteenth Dynasty of Egypt|en|Eighteenth Dynasty of Egypt|WD=Q146055|lt=18th Dynasty|1=Eighteenth Dynasty of Egypt}}, the {{ill|High Priest of Ptah|de|Wer-cherep-hemut|ja|プタハの大司祭|zh|普塔赫大祭司|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} also bore the title Sem priest, identifying with {{ill|Iunmutef|1=Iunmutef|2=de|3=Iunmutef|4=es|5=Iunmutef|6=fr|7=Iounmoutef|8=fi|9=Inmutef|de|Iunmutef|1=Iunmutef|2=de|3=Iunmutef|4=es|5=Iunmutef|6=fr|7=Iounmoutef|8=fi|9=Inmutef|ja|イウンムテフ|zh|尤努穆特夫|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}. Their garb gained the panther pelt and sidelock. Under {{ill|Amenhotep III|en|Amenhotep III|WD=Q42606|lt=Amenhotep III|1=Amenhotep III}}, we see the earliest Memphite high-priest title extended with Sem status: a statue of Ptahmose (“Image of Iunmutef”) son of Mencheper. The Sem priest also adopted the “King’s Son” title of {{ill|Horus Iunmutef|de|Horus Iunmutef|ja|ホルス・イウンムテフ|zh|荷鲁斯-尤努穆特夫|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}. Originally, in the {{ill|Old Kingdom of Egypt|en|Old Kingdom of Egypt|WD=Q177819|lt=Old Kingdom|1=Old Kingdom of Egypt}}, this role was reserved for the king’s eldest son; later, the Sem priest embodied the king’s son as “Observer at the ritual purification of the deceased,” linking directly to the deity {{ill|SEM|en|SEM|WD=Q233287|lt=Sem|1=SEM}}. === Activities of the Sem priest === The Sem priest officiated the {{ill|Opening of the mouth ceremony|en|Opening of the mouth ceremony|WD=Q1191513|lt=Opening of the mouth ceremony|1=Opening of the mouth ceremony}}, depicted in panther pelt, and mediated between king and gods. He dressed the king, allocated offerings, and during {{ill|Sed festival|en|Sed festival|WD=Q604644|lt=Sed festival|1=Sed festival}} processions renewed royal power. In the temple vestibule of {{ill|Pepi II Neferkare|en|Pepi II Neferkare|WD=Q6198|lt=Pepi II|1=Pepi II Neferkare}}, the west wall shows the Sem offering linen, incense, and oil to the shrine gods, confirming the king as “First of the living”: {{Quote|The gods come, bringing ‘life, power, and health for the son of his body Nebmaatre’ and promise: ‘He celebrates the New Year festival with a broad heart, renews the months in the Nehebkau festival, and performs many Sed festivals on the throne like Re.’|West wall of Pepi II|ref=Gustave Jéquier: ''Le monument funéraire de Pepi II, Service des Antiquités de l'Égypte, Bd. 2: Le temple''. Institut Français d'Archéologie Orientale, Cairo 1938, Plates 50 & 52.}} In Greco-Roman liturgy, {{ill|Seth (disambiguation)|en|Seth (disambiguation)|WD=Q971389|lt=Seth|1=Seth (disambiguation)}} fled {{ill|Thoth|en|Thoth|WD=Q146921|lt=Thoth|1=Thoth}} and {{ill|Anubis|en|Anubis|WD=Q47534|lt=Anubis|1=Anubis}}, shape-shifted into a panther, and was slain by Anubis. Anubis then entered Osiris’s embalming hall wearing the flayed pelt, declaring: “Seth is here.” == See also == * {{ill|Sem-em-iritef|de|Sem-em-iritef|ja|Sem-em-iritef|zh|Sem-em-iritef|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} * {{ill|Setem-atef-Usir|de|Setem-atef-Usir|ja|Setem-atef-Usir|zh|Setem-atef-Usir|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} * {{ill|Min festival|en|Min festival|WD=Q1936130|lt=Min-Fest|1=Min festival}} * {{ill|Ancient Egyptian administrative and functional titles|de|Altägyptische Beamten- und Funktionstitel|ja|古代エジプトの行政・官職称号|zh|古埃及行政与职能头衔|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} == Literature == * Friedrich-Wilhelm Bissing, Hermann Kees: ''Das Re-Heiligtum des Königs Ne-Woser-Re (Rathures), Bd. 2''. Duncker, Berlin 1923, Plates 13, 18; pp. 68–69. * Hermann Kees: ''Das Priestertum im ägyptischen Staat vom Neuen Reich bis zur Spätzeit''. Brill, Leiden 1953. * Christian Leitz et al.: ''Lexikon der ägyptischen Götter und Götterbezeichnungen''. Bd. 6: “H̱ - s”. Peeters, Leuven 2002, ISBN 90-429-1151-4, p. 306. * Flinders Petrie: ''Abydos I + II''. Brian Yare Egyptology, Worcester 2005 (Reprint 1902). * Heinrich Schäfer: ''Die Mysterien des Osiris in Abydos unter König Sesostris III – Nach dem Denkstein …''. Hinrichs, Leipzig 1904, pp. 15–19. * William A. Ward: ''Index of Egyptian administrative and religious titles of the Middle Kingdom: with a glossary …''. American University of Beirut, Beirut 1982, p. 168, No. 1465. == Weblinks == * [http://www.egyptology.com/reeder/enigma/tekenu8.html The Sem priest as panther-pelt wielder] == References == [[fr:Pr%C3%AAtre-sem]] [[es:Sacerdote sem]] [[de:Sem (Priester)]][[ja:セム祭司]][[zh:瑟姆祭司]] [[ja:セムとムトネフェル]][[de:Sem und Mut-nefer]] {{translated page|de|Sem (Priester)|version=223454035|comment=Imported full de history, revisions duplicated and version not here due to an error with my bot related to the German being under the same pagename as the english}} [[fr:Prêtre-sem]] {{wikidata link|Q1692364}} [[Category:Priest Titles (Ancient Egypt)]] [[Category:Priestertitel (Altes Ägypten)]] [[Category:Wikidata no short description]] [[Category:WikiProject Ancient Egypt]] [[Category:pages]]

Originally, in the {{ill|Old Kingdom of Egypt|en|Old Kingdom of Egypt|WD=Q177819|lt=Old Kingdom|1=Old Kingdom of Egypt}}, this role was reserved for the king’s eldest son; later, the Sem priest embodied the king’s son as “Observer at the ritual purification of the deceased,” linking directly to the deity {{ill|SEM|en|SEM|WD=Q233287|lt=Sem|1=SEM}}. === Activities of the Sem priest === The Sem priest officiated the {{ill|Opening of the mouth ceremony|en|Opening of the mouth ceremony|WD=Q1191513|lt=Opening of the mouth ceremony|1=Opening of the mouth ceremony}}, depicted in panther pelt, and mediated between king and gods. He dressed the king, allocated offerings, and during {{ill|Sed festival|en|Sed festival|WD=Q604644|lt=Sed festival|1=Sed festival}} processions renewed royal power. In the temple vestibule of {{ill|Pepi II Neferkare|en|Pepi II Neferkare|WD=Q6198|lt=Pepi II|1=Pepi II Neferkare}}, the west wall shows the Sem offering linen, incense, and oil to the shrine gods, confirming the king as “First of the living”: {{Quote|The gods come, bringing ‘life, power, and health for the son of his body Nebmaatre’ and promise: ‘He celebrates the New Year festival with a broad heart, renews the months in the Nehebkau festival, and performs many Sed festivals on the throne like Re.’|West wall of Pepi II|ref=Gustave Jéquier: ''Le monument funéraire de Pepi II, Service des Antiquités de l'Égypte, Bd. 2: Le temple''. Institut Français d'Archéologie Orientale, Cairo 1938, Plates 50 & 52.}} In Greco-Roman liturgy, {{ill|Seth (disambiguation)|en|Seth (disambiguation)|WD=Q971389|lt=Seth|1=Seth (disambiguation)}} fled {{ill|Thoth|en|Thoth|WD=Q146921|lt=Thoth|1=Thoth}} and {{ill|Anubis|en|Anubis|WD=Q47534|lt=Anubis|1=Anubis}}, shape-shifted into a panther, and was slain by Anubis. Anubis then entered Osiris’s embalming hall wearing the flayed pelt, declaring: “Seth is here.” == See also == * {{ill|Sem-em-iritef|de|Sem-em-iritef|ja|Sem-em-iritef|zh|Sem-em-iritef|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} * {{ill|Setem-atef-Usir|de|Setem-atef-Usir|ja|Setem-atef-Usir|zh|Setem-atef-Usir|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} * {{ill|Min festival|en|Min festival|WD=Q1936130|lt=Min-Fest|1=Min festival}} * {{ill|Ancient Egyptian administrative and functional titles|de|Altägyptische Beamten- und Funktionstitel|ja|古代エジプトの行政・官職称号|zh|古埃及行政与职能头衔|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} == Literature == * Friedrich-Wilhelm Bissing, Hermann Kees: ''Das Re-Heiligtum des Königs Ne-Woser-Re (Rathures), Bd. 2''.
